name: DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON

topics.title: Introduction to Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET865 - DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Python # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Python > [!IMPORTANT] > **Python** is a **high-level, interpreted, object-oriented, dynamically-typed, and general-purpose programming language** designed by *Guido van Rossum* and first released in **1991**. It emphasizes **code readability** through significant indentation and a clean, English-like syntax, making it one of the most adopted languages in **Data Analytics, Machine Learning, Web Development, Automation, and Scientific Computing**. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEIET865)**, Python serves as the foundational computational tool for the entire course, acting as the **primary interface** between theoretical algorithms and practical data-driven implementations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Recipe Book" Imagine you are writing a **recipe book** for a friend who is an expert chef but has **never seen your ingredients before**. - A **low-level language** (like C or Assembly) is like telling the chef: *"Take RAM address 0x7FFE3A, move 5 bytes to register AL, then add 1"* — fast, but exhausting. - **Python** is like saying: *"Add 5 eggs to the bowl and stir gently."* The interpreter (chef's brain) **figures out the rest**. This **abstraction layer** is what makes Python ideal for engineering students focusing on **problem-solving** rather than memory management. ## 1.3 Key Features of Python (KTU Board-Listed) > [!NOTE] > **Top 10 Salient Features — Memorize for 3-Mark Questions** 1. **Easy to Learn & Use** — Minimal keywords, simple syntax. 2. **Interpreted Language** — Executes line-by-line; no separate compilation step. 3. **Cross-Platform** — Runs on **Windows, Linux, macOS, Raspberry Pi**. 4. **Free and Open Source** — Python is distributed under the **PSF (Python Software Foundation) License**, which is GPL-compatible. 5. **Object-Oriented** — Supports classes, inheritance, encapsulation, polymorphism. 6. **Large Standard Library** — "Batteries included" philosophy; **>200,000 third-party packages** on **PyPI (Python Package Index)**. 7. **Dynamically Typed** — No need to declare variable types explicitly. 8. **High-Level Language** — No memory management required from the programmer. 9. **Extensible & Embeddable** — Can integrate with C, C++, Java. 10. **GUI Programming Support** — Tkinter, PyQt, Kivy for graphical interfaces. ## 1.4 Python Applications in Engineering > [!TIP] > **Where will YOU use Python in your B.Tech project?** > - **Data Analytics** → Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib > - **Machine Learning** → Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch > - **IoT & Embedded** → MicroPython on Raspberry Pi Pico > - **Web Scraping** → BeautifulSoup, Scrapy > - **Automation** → Selenium, PyAutoGUI ## 1.5 Why Python for Data Analytics & ML? > [!IMPORTANT] > Python is the **de-facto industry standard** for Data Science because of: > - **Readability** → Researchers publish reproducible code easily. > - **Ecosystem** → NumPy (numerical), Pandas (tabular), Matplotlib (plotting), Scikit-learn (ML). > - **Community** → Largest programming community on **Stack Overflow** (over **2 million** Python-tagged questions). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Python Execution Architecture — The "Behind the Scenes" Python code does **not** run directly on your hardware. It passes through a **layered translation system**: **Step 1 → Source Code (.py)** You write human-readable code in a file like `hello.py`. **Step 2 → Bytecode (.pyc)** The **Compiler (part of the interpreter)** converts your `.py` file into an intermediate representation called **bytecode**, stored in the `__pycache__` folder. Bytecode is a **platform-independent, low-level set of instructions** for the **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)**. **Step 3 → Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** The **PVM** is the runtime engine that interprets bytecode and translates it into **machine code (0s and 1s)** that your CPU executes. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important for KTU exams?** > Because Python is **interpreted**, debugging is line-by-line. There is **no separate `.exe` generation** in CPython (the standard implementation). ## 2.2 Python Identifiers, Keywords, and Variables ### 2.2.1 Identifiers Identifiers are **names** given to variables, functions, classes, modules, etc. **Rules for Valid Identifiers (Board Favorite — 3 Marks):** 1. Must **start with a letter (A–Z, a–z)** or an **underscore (`_`)**. 2. Subsequent characters can be **letters, digits (0–9), or underscores**. 3. **Cannot be a keyword** (reserved word). 4. **Case-sensitive** — `Age` and `age` are different identifiers. 5. **No special characters** allowed: `@, #, $, %, !` are forbidden. ### 2.2.2 Python Keywords (Total: **35 keywords** in Python 3.x) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these 35 keywords** — frequently asked in 3-mark questions. | Category | Keywords | |----------|----------| | Boolean | `True`, `False`, `None` | | Logical | `and`, `or`, `not`, `in`, `is` | | Conditional | `if`, `elif`, `else` | | Loops | `for`, `while`, `break`, `continue`, `pass` | | Function | `def`, `return`, `lambda`, `yield` | | Class | `class`, `self` | | Exception | `try`, `except`, `finally`, `raise`, `assert` | | Import | `import`, `from`, `as` | | Scope | `global`, `nonlocal` | | Async | `async`, `await` | | Other | `with`, `del` | ### 2.2.3 Variables A variable is a **named memory location** that stores a value. Python uses **dynamic typing** — type is inferred at runtime. ```python x = 10 # int name = "KTU" # str pi = 3.14 # float is_valid = True # bool ``` ## 2.3 Python Operators — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!WARNING] > **Avoid using `|` directly in table cells** — LaTeX rendering breaks. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. | Operator Type | Symbols | Example | Result | |---------------|---------|---------|--------| | **Arithmetic** | `+ - * / % // **` | `7 \vert\vert 2` (floor div) | `3` | | **Comparison** | `== != > < >= <=` | `5 > 3` | `True` | | **Logical** | `and, or, not` | `True and False` | `False` | | **Bitwise** | `& \vert ^ ~ << >>` | `5 & 3` | `1` | | **Assignment** | `= += -= *= /= %= **= //=` | `x += 2` | `x = x+2` | | **Identity** | `is, is not` | `x is y` | checks identity | | **Membership** | `in, not in` | `5 in [1,2,3]` | `False` | ### 2.3.1 Operator Precedence (Highest → Lowest) | Precedence | Operator | Description | |-----------:|----------|-------------| | 1 | `()` | Parentheses | | 2 | `**` | Exponentiation | | 3 | `+x, -x, ~x` | Unary | | 4 | `*, /, //, %` | Multiplicative | | 5 | `+, -` | Additive | | 6 | `<<, >>` | Bitwise Shift | | 7 | `&` | Bitwise AND | | 8 | `^` | Bitwise XOR | | 9 | `\vert` | Bitwise OR | | 10 | `==, !=, >, <, >=, <=` | Comparisons | | 11 | `not` | Logical NOT | | 12 | `and` | Logical AND | | 13 | `or` | Logical OR | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > In **production ML pipelines**, Python's operator overloading (via **dunder/magic methods** like `__add__`) allows NumPy arrays to use `+` for vector addition — a feature crucial for **matrix operations** in neural networks. For example, `A + B` on two NumPy arrays performs **element-wise addition** across thousands of dimensions in a single line. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Logical Flow: How Python Executes a Simple Program Let us trace what happens when you run the following program: ```python # program: area.py radius = 5 pi = 3.14159 area = pi * radius ** 2 print("Area =", area) ``` ### Step-by-Step Analysis **Step 1 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)** The interpreter scans the source code and breaks it into **tokens**: $$\text{tokens} = \{\text{"radius"}, "=", "5", \text{"pi"}, "=", "3.14159", \text{"area"}, "=", \text{"pi"}, "*", \text{"radius"}, "**", "2"\}$$ **Step 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing)** Tokens are arranged into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. The expression `pi * radius ** 2` becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expression} &\rightarrow \text{BinaryOp}(*, \text{Left}, \text{Right}) \\ \text{Left} &\rightarrow \text{Name}(pi) \\ \text{Right} &\rightarrow \text{BinaryOp}(**, \text{Left2}, \text{Right2}) \\ \text{Left2} &\rightarrow \text{Name}(radius) \\ \text{Right2} &\rightarrow \text{Constant}(2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Bytecode Generation** The AST is compiled to **bytecode instructions** for the PVM. Approximate bytecode: ``` LOAD_FAST radius LOAD_CONST 2 BINARY_POWER LOAD_GLOBAL pi BINARY_MULTIPLY STORE_NAME area ``` **Step 4 — Execution by PVM** The PVM executes each bytecode instruction sequentially: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack: } &\text{push radius} \rightarrow 5 \\ &\text{push 2} \rightarrow 5, 2 \\ &\text{BINARY\_POWER} \rightarrow 25 \\ &\text{push pi} \rightarrow 25, 3.14159 \\ &\text{BINARY\_MULTIPLY} \rightarrow 78.53975 \\ &\text{STORE\_NAME area} \rightarrow \text{area} = 78.53975 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Output** `print()` is a **built-in function** that converts `area` to a string and writes it to `sys.stdout`: $$\text{Output:} \quad \text{"Area = 78.53975"}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Operator Precedence in `2 + 3 * 4 ** 2` Let us evaluate step-by-step using the precedence table from Section 2.3.1. **Original expression:** $$E = 2 + 3 \times 4^{2}$$ **Step 1 — Apply exponentiation (`**`) first (precedence 2):** $$E = 2 + 3 \times 16$$ **Step 2 — Apply multiplication (`*`) next (precedence 4):** $$E = 2 + 48$$ **Step 3 — Apply addition (`+`) last (precedence 5):** $$E = 50$$ **Final Result: 50** > [!NOTE] > If the expression were written **without** precedence (i.e., strictly left-to-right), the answer would have been `100` (because `(2+3) * 4**2 = 5*16 = 80`, then `+2` if we did linear) — showing **why precedence matters**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dynamic Type Inference When you write `x = 42`, Python performs the following logical deduction: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Observation:} \quad &x = 42 \\ \text{Token type:} \quad &42 \in \mathbb{Z} \\ \text{Python internal call:} \quad &\text{type}(x) \\ \text{Result:} \quad &x \rightarrow \text{class 'int'} \\ \text{Memory:} \quad &\text{id}(x) = 140234567890112 \text{ (unique address)} \end{aligned} $$ **Variable reassignment changes type dynamically:** $$ \begin{aligned} x &= 42 \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{type} = \text{int} \\ x &= \text{"Hello"} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{type} = \text{str} \\ x &= [1, 2, 3] \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{type} = \text{list} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is **duck typing** in action: *"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck."* Python cares about **behavior**, not declared type. ## 3.4 Memory Model: Reference Counting Every Python object has an **internal reference counter**. When `x = obj` is executed: $$ \text{ref\_count}(obj) \mathrel{+}= 1 $$ When a reference is removed or reassigned: $$ \text{ref\_count}(obj) \mathrel{-}= 1 $$ When `ref_count(obj) == 0`, the **Garbage Collector (GC)** reclaims the memory. **Example walkthrough:** $$ \begin{aligned} a &= [1, 2, 3] \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{ref\_count}(\text{list}) = 1 \\ b &= a \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{ref\_count}(\text{list}) = 2 \\ \text{del } a \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{ref\_count}(\text{list}) = 1 \\ \text{del } b \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{ref\_count}(\text{list}) = 0 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{GC collects} \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Python Execution Flow — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Writes Source Code file.py] --> B[Lexical Analyzer Tokenizer] B --> C[Syntax Analyzer Parser] C --> D[Abstract Syntax Tree AST] D --> E[Bytecode Compiler] E --> F[Bytecode file.pyc cached in pycache] F --> G[Python Virtual Machine PVM Interpreter] G --> H[Machine Code 0s and 1s] H --> I[CPU Executes Instructions] I --> J[Output to Console or File] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Python Interpreter Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC[Source Phase] P1[Programmer Code .py] end subgraph CMP[Compilation Phase] P2[Tokenizer] P3[Parser generates AST] P4[Bytecode Generator] end subgraph RUN[Runtime Phase] P5[Interpreter Loop] P6[Memory Manager Garbage Collector] P7[Built in Modules sys os math] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P5 --> P7 P5 --> P8[Final Output] style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P5 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P7 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Python Application Domain Map ```mermaid mindmap root((Python
Applications)) DataAnalytics NumPy Pandas Matplotlib Seaborn MachineLearning Scikit-learn TensorFlow PyTorch Keras WebDevelopment Django Flask FastAPI Automation Selenium PyAutoGUI OS scripts Scientific SciPy SymPy IoT MicroPython Raspberry Pi ``` ## 4.4 Identifier Naming Convention Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Python Identifier] --> B{Starts with?} B -->|Letter A-Z a-z| C{Contains only valid chars?} B -->|Underscore _| C B -->|Digit 0-9| X1[INVALID SyntaxError] B -->|Special @ # $| X1 C -->|Letters Digits Underscores| D{Is a keyword?} C -->|Other special chars| X2[INVALID SyntaxError] D -->|Yes| X3[INVALID Cannot use reserved word] D -->|No| E[VALID Identifier] E --> F[Convention snake_case for variables] E --> G[Convention PascalCase for classes] E --> H[Convention ALL_CAPS for constants] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style X1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style X2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style X3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define Python. List any FIVE features of Python.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Python is a **high-level, interpreted, object-oriented, dynamically-typed, and general-purpose** programming language developed by **Guido van Rossum** and released in **1991**. It emphasizes code readability and uses significant indentation to define code blocks. **Five Features (½ Mark Each = 2.5 Marks):** 1. **Easy to Learn and Use** — Simple English-like syntax. 2. **Interpreted** — No compilation step; line-by-line execution. 3. **Cross-Platform** — Runs on Windows, Linux, macOS. 4. **Dynamically Typed** — Type checking at runtime; no need to declare types. 5. **Large Standard Library** — Over 200,000 packages on PyPI for diverse tasks. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Explain the difference between a keyword and an identifier in Python with examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Keyword (1.5 Marks) | Identifier (1.5 Marks) | |--------|---------------------|------------------------| | **Definition** | Reserved words with predefined meaning in Python | User-defined names for variables, functions, classes | | **Case** | Always lowercase (except `True`, `False`, `None`) | Case-sensitive | | **Total Count** | 35 in Python 3.x | Unlimited (user-defined) | | **Examples** | `if`, `else`, `for`, `while`, `class`, `return` | `student_name`, `total_marks`, `MyClass` | | **Can be redefined?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (but not recommended) | | **Syntax to check** | `keyword.kwlist` | User-defined | **Example:** ```python # 'for' is a keyword, 'i' is an identifier for i in range(5): print(i) ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** **a) Explain the Python execution model in detail. Describe the role of the compiler, bytecode, and the Python Virtual Machine (PVM) with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Overview (2 Marks):** Python follows a **two-step execution model**: first, the source code (`.py`) is compiled to **bytecode** (a platform-independent intermediate representation), and then the **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** interprets this bytecode into machine instructions for the CPU. **(ii) Compilation Phase (2 Marks):** The **Python compiler** performs three sub-tasks: 1. **Lexical Analysis** — Source code is broken into tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals). 2. **Syntax Analysis** — Tokens are arranged into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** that represents the program's grammatical structure. 3. **Bytecode Generation** — The AST is converted into low-level instructions called **bytecode**, stored in a `.pyc` file inside the `__pycache__` folder for faster re-execution. **(iii) PVM Execution Phase (2 Marks):** The **PVM** is a **software-based interpreter** (a loop running in C) that: - Reads bytecode instructions one by one. - Uses a **stack-based execution model** to perform operations. - Manages memory through **reference counting and garbage collection**. - Invokes built-in modules like `sys`, `os`, and `math` as needed. **(iv) Diagram (1 Mark):** ``` Source Code (.py) → Compiler → Bytecode (.pyc) → PVM → Machine Code → CPU → Output ``` **(v) Real-world significance (Bonus):** This is why Python is **slower than C** (extra interpretation layer) but **more portable** (write once, run on any OS with Python installed). --- **b) With suitable examples, explain the different categories of operators in Python. Also, demonstrate operator precedence by evaluating the expression `4 + 3 * 2 ** 2 - 1`. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Operator Categories (4 Marks):** | Category | Operators | Example | Output | |----------|-----------|---------|--------| | Arithmetic | `+ - * / % // **` | `10 // 3` | `3` | | Comparison | `== != > < >= <=` | `5 >= 5` | `True` | | Logical | `and, or, not` | `True and False` | `False` | | Bitwise | `& \| ^ ~ << >>` | `5 & 3` | `1` | | Assignment | `= += -= *=` | `x = 5; x += 2` | `x = 7` | | Identity | `is, is not` | `x is y` | compares identity | | Membership | `in, not in` | `'a' in 'cat'` | `True` | **Examples:** ```python a, b = 10, 3 print(a + b) # 13 (Arithmetic) print(a > b) # True (Comparison) print(a > 5 and b < 5) # True (Logical) print(a & b) # 2 (Bitwise AND) print(a is not b) # True (Identity) print(2 in [1,2,3]) # True (Membership) ``` **(ii) Operator Precedence Evaluation (3 Marks):** Given expression: `4 + 3 * 2 ** 2 - 1` **Step 1** — Apply `**` (precedence 2): $$4 + 3 \times 4 - 1$$ **Step 2** — Apply `*` (precedence 4): $$4 + 12 - 1$$ **Step 3** — Apply `+` and `-` (left-to-right, precedence 5): $$16 - 1 = 15$$ $$\boxed{\text{Final Result} = 15}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **a) Discuss the rules for naming identifiers in Python. Identify which of the following are valid identifiers and justify your answer: `2num`, `_value`, `my-name`, `class`, `Roll_No`, `π`, `total$marks`, `if`. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Identifier Rules (3 Marks):** 1. Must begin with a **letter (A–Z, a–z)** or an **underscore (`_`)**. 2. Subsequent characters may include **letters, digits (0–9), underscores**. 3. **Cannot be a keyword** (reserved word). 4. **Case-sensitive** — `Name` ≠ `name`. 5. **No special characters** like `@, #, $, %, !, -`. 6. No length limit, but should be **meaningful** (`student_age` not `sa`). **(ii) Validation Table (4 Marks):** | Identifier | Valid? | Reason | |------------|--------|--------| | `2num` | ❌ Invalid | Starts with a digit | | `_value` | ✅ Valid | Starts with underscore | | `my-name` | ❌ Invalid | Contains hyphen (`-`) | | `class` | ❌ Invalid | Reserved keyword | | `Roll_No` | ✅ Valid | Letters, digit, underscore | | `π` | ⚠️ Discouraged | Non-ASCII (works in Python 3, but not portable) | | `total$marks` | ❌ Invalid | Contains `$` | | `if` | ❌ Invalid | Reserved keyword | **Conclusion:** Only `_value` and `Roll_No` are **fully valid and recommended**. --- **b) What is a variable in Python? Explain dynamic typing with an example. How does Python manage memory using reference counting? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **(i) Variable (1.5 Marks):** A **variable** in Python is a **named reference to an object stored in memory**. It does not store the value directly (like C); instead, it points to an object created in the heap. ```python x = 100 # x is a name; 100 is an int object ``` **(ii) Dynamic Typing (2.5 Marks):** Python does not require explicit type declaration. The type is **inferred at runtime** based on the assigned value, and a variable can be reassigned to a different type later. ```python x = 10 # type(x) → x = "Hello KTU" # type(x) → x = [1, 2, 3] # type(x) → x = 3.14 # type(x) → ``` This behavior is called **duck typing** — the variable's type is determined by its current value, not declared in advance. **(iii) Memory Management via Reference Counting (3 Marks):** Every Python object has an **internal reference counter** that tracks how many names point to it. | Operation | Effect on `ref_count` | |-----------|----------------------| | `obj = MyClass()` | ref_count = 1 | | `ref = obj` | ref_count = 2 | | `del obj` | ref_count = 1 | | `del ref` | ref_count = 0 → **Garbage Collector reclaims memory** | **Example trace:** ```python a = [1, 2, 3] # ref_count of list = 1 b = a # ref_count = 2 del a # ref_count = 1 del b # ref_count = 0 → memory freed ``` Python also uses a **cyclic garbage collector** to detect reference cycles (e.g., lists referencing each other) that simple ref-counting cannot handle. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing keywords with built-in functions** — `print` is a built-in function, NOT a keyword. Don't write "`print` is a keyword" in exams. > 2. **Forgetting the count of keywords** — There are **35 keywords** in Python 3.x. Writing "32" or "33" loses ½ mark. > 3. **Ignoring case-sensitivity** — `True` and `true` are different. Only `True`, `False`, `None` are capitalized keywords. > 4. **Operator precedence questions** — Always show **step-by-step evaluation**. Writing only the final answer (e.g., "15") without showing `** → * → +/-` steps loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Forgetting the PVM** — Many students describe only the compiler. Examiners expect **both** compilation and interpretation phases for full marks. > 6. **Writing "Python is compiled"** — WRONG. Python is **interpreted** (technically, it is compiled to bytecode, then interpreted by PVM). Be precise. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):** - ✅ Python is **interpreted, high-level, object-oriented, dynamically typed**, and **general-purpose**. - ✅ Created by **Guido van Rossum**; first released in **1991**; current stable: **Python 3.x**. - ✅ Python has **35 keywords** — all lowercase except `True`, `False`, `None`. - ✅ Identifiers: start with **letter or `_`**, no special chars, case-sensitive, no keywords. - ✅ Python execution = **Source → Bytecode → PVM → Machine Code → CPU**. - ✅ Variable in Python is a **reference to an object**, not a memory cell holding the value directly. - ✅ **Dynamic typing** = type inferred at runtime, can be reassigned to any type. - ✅ **Duck typing** = behavior matters, not declared type. - ✅ Operator precedence (high → low): `**` → unary → `* / // %` → `+ -` → shifts → bitwise → comparisons → `not` → `and` → `or`. - ✅ Memory management: **reference counting** + **cyclic garbage collector**. - ✅ Python file extension: **`.py`**; cached bytecode: **`.pyc`** in `__pycache__`. - ✅ The official Python package repository is **PyPI (Python Package Index)**. - ✅ Python's "batteries included" philosophy refers to its **large standard library**. - ✅ KTU expects both **theoretical explanation + code snippet + output** for full marks in 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Using Pandas for Data Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET865 - DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Using Pandas for Data Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pandas** is an open-source, BSD-licensed Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. It is the de-facto standard library for tabular data manipulation in Python and forms the foundational layer upon which nearly every data analytics and machine learning pipeline in the Python ecosystem is constructed.

Built on top of the **NumPy** numerical computing library, Pandas introduces two primary data structures designed for handling labeled, relational, and heterogeneous data:

1. **`Series`** – A **one-dimensional labeled array** capable of holding any data type (integers, strings, floats, Python objects, etc.).
2. **`DataFrame`** – A **two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular data structure** with labeled axes (rows and columns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**  
> Pandas is described in the KTU 2024 scheme as *"a fast, powerful, flexible and easy to use open-source data analysis and manipulation tool, built on top of the Python programming language."* It is officially positioned in **Module 1** as the gateway library for the rest of the Data Analytics and Machine Learning coursework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Pandas as a **supercharged Excel spreadsheet living inside Python**.

- Imagine an Excel workbook with multiple sheets. Each sheet has **column headers** (labels) and **row indices**. Pandas' `DataFrame` is exactly this — but with the entire computational power of Python at your fingertips.
- Imagine a **single column of an Excel sheet** with a name. That is what a Pandas `Series` is — a named, indexed list of values.

**Real-World Analogy — The Library System:**

| Library Concept | Pandas Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| A catalog card index | `Index` | Labels for rows (lookup keys) |
| A single shelf of books | `Series` | 1D labeled data |
| An entire library section | `DataFrame` | 2D tabular data |
| The librarian | Pandas Functions (`merge`, `groupby`, `pivot`) | Data manipulation operations |

In essence, if NumPy is the **calculator of Python**, Pandas is the **spreadsheet + database engine of Python**.

## 1.3 Why Pandas Exists — The Motivation

Before Pandas, data scientists used:
- **Pure Python lists/dictionaries** → Slow, no vectorization, no built-in alignment.
- **NumPy arrays** → Fast, but cannot handle **labeled data** or **heterogeneous types** gracefully.

Pandas solved this by introducing:
- **Label-based indexing** (`loc`, `iloc`)
- **Built-in handling of missing data** (`NaN`, `None`)
- **Heterogeneous columns** (one column `int`, next column `string`)
- **Powerful group-by, merge, join, pivot, and reshape** operations
- **Direct I/O with CSV, Excel, SQL, JSON, Parquet**

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Benchmark:** Pandas operations are implemented in **Cython** and C under the hood, making vectorized Pandas operations typically **10x to 100x faster** than equivalent pure Python `for`-loop implementations on datasets with thousands of rows.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Core Data Structures

### 2.1.1 Pandas `Series`

A `Series` is a **one-dimensional labeled array** that can hold any data type. It is built on top of a NumPy array but adds an **index** as a label.

**Structural Anatomy:**
$$\text{Series} = \text{ndarray (values)} + \text{Index (labels)}$$

Formally:
$$\text{Series}(s) = \{(i, v_i) \mid i \in I,\ v_i \in V\}$$
where $I$ is the Index set and $V$ is the value array.

**Construction:**
```python
import pandas as pd
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30, 40], index=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'], name='Scores')
print(s)
```

### 2.1.2 Pandas `DataFrame`

A `DataFrame` is a **two-dimensional labeled data structure** with columns of potentially different types. It can be thought of as:
- A dictionary of `Series` objects (each column is a Series).
- A 2D NumPy array with row and column labels.

**Structural Anatomy:**
$$\text{DataFrame}(D) = \{(\text{row}_i, \text{col}_j) \rightarrow v_{ij} \mid i \in I_R,\ j \in I_C\}$$
where $I_R$ = row index, $I_C$ = column index.

**Construction:**
```python
data = {
    'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'],
    'Age':  [25, 30, 35],
    'City': ['Kochi', 'Trivandrum', 'Calicut']
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data, index=['E1', 'E2', 'E3'])
```

## 2.2 The Index — The Backbone of Pandas

The `Index` object is an **immutable, ordered, sliceable set of labels**. It enables:
- **Label-based alignment** (automatic matching of labels during arithmetic).
- **Fast lookup** (O(1) hash-table based lookups for unique indices).
- **Hierarchical indexing** (MultiIndex for multi-dimensional data in 2D form).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Index matters:** When you do `df1 + df2`, Pandas **aligns rows AND columns by their index labels** before performing the operation. Misaligned labels produce `NaN`. This is fundamentally different from NumPy, where operations are purely positional.

## 2.3 Reading and Writing Data (I/O)

Pandas supports an extensive range of file formats via reader/writer functions:

| Format | Reader Function | Writer Function | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CSV** | `pd.read_csv()` | `df.to_csv()` | Most common tabular data exchange |
| **Excel** | `pd.read_excel()` | `df.to_excel()` | Business reports |
| **JSON** | `pd.read_json()` | `df.to_json()` | Web APIs, NoSQL data |
| **SQL** | `pd.read_sql()` | `df.to_sql()` | Database integration |
| **Parquet** | `pd.read_parquet()` | `df.to_parquet()` | Columnar storage, Big Data |
| **HTML** | `pd.read_html()` | `df.to_html()` | Web scraping |

**Most-used KTU-relevant reader:**
```python
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv',
                 sep=',',
                 header=0,           # First row = column names
                 index_col='ID',     # Use 'ID' column as row index
                 na_values=['NA', '?'])  # Treat these as NaN
```

## 2.4 Core Data Manipulation Operations

### 2.4.1 Indexing & Selection

| Method | Syntax | Selection Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df[col]` | By column label | Single column → Series | `df['Name']` |
| `df.col` | Attribute access | Single column → Series | `df.Name` |
| `df.loc[]` | By **label** | Row/column by label | `df.loc['E1', 'Age']` |
| `df.iloc[]` | By **integer position** | Row/column by position | `df.iloc[0, 1]` |
| `df.at[]` | Scalar by label | Fast scalar access | `df.at['E1', 'Age']` |
| `df.iat[]` | Scalar by position | Fast scalar scalar | `df.iat[0, 1]` |

### 2.4.2 Filtering (Boolean Indexing)

$$F = \{r_i \in \text{rows}(D) \mid \text{cond}(r_i) = \text{True}\}$$

```python
# Filter rows where Age > 25 AND City == 'Kochi'
filtered = df[(df['Age'] > 25) & (df['City'] == 'Kochi')]
```

### 2.4.3 Handling Missing Data

| Operation | Function | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Detect missing | `df.isnull()` / `df.isna()` | Returns Boolean DataFrame |
| Drop missing | `df.dropna(axis=0)` | Drop rows with NaN |
| Fill missing | `df.fillna(value)` | Replace NaN with value |
| Forward fill | `df.ffill()` | Propagate last valid value |
| Backward fill | `df.bfill()` | Use next valid value |

### 2.4.4 Adding/Modifying Columns

```python
# Vectorized arithmetic (column-wise)
df['Age_in_5_years'] = df['Age'] + 5

# Conditional column creation
df['Senior'] = df['Age'].apply(lambda x: 'Yes' if x >= 60 else 'No')
```

### 2.4.5 Sorting

```python
df.sort_values(by='Age', ascending=False, inplace=True)   # Sort by column
df.sort_index(ascending=True, inplace=True)               # Sort by index
```

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Pandas Syntax | Returns | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create Series | `pd.Series(data, index=idx)` | `Series` | $O(n)$ |
| Create DataFrame | `pd.DataFrame(dict)` | `DataFrame` | $O(n \cdot m)$ |
| Read CSV | `pd.read_csv(path)` | `DataFrame` | $O(n)$ |
| Inspect head | `df.head(k)` | `DataFrame` | $O(k)$ |
| Shape | `df.shape` | Tuple `(rows, cols)` | $O(1)$ |
| Column types | `df.dtypes` | Series of dtypes | $O(1)$ |
| Summary stats | `df.describe()` | `DataFrame` | $O(n \cdot m)$ |
| Select column | `df['col']` | `Series` | $O(1)$ |
| Boolean filter | `df[cond]` | `DataFrame` | $O(n)$ |
| `.loc[]` label | `df.loc[idx]` | Series/DataFrame | $O(1)$ hash |
| `.iloc[]` position | `df.iloc[pos]` | Series/DataFrame | $O(1)$ |
| Drop rows | `df.dropna()` | `DataFrame` | $O(n)$ |
| Fill NaN | `df.fillna(v)` | `DataFrame` | $O(n)$ |
| Apply function | `df.apply(f)` | `DataFrame` | $O(n \cdot m)$ |
| Map values | `df['col'].map(f)` | `Series` | $O(n)$ |
| Sort by value | `df.sort_values(by)` | `DataFrame` | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Unique values | `df['col'].unique()` | ndarray | $O(n)$ |
| Value counts | `df['col'].value_counts()` | `Series` | $O(n)$ |
| Concatenate | `pd.concat([d1, d2])` | `DataFrame` | $O(n_1 + n_2)$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pandas is extensively used in:
- **Civil Engineering:** Analyzing sensor data from structural health monitoring systems.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Time-series analysis of power grid load data.
- **Computer Science:** Pre-processing datasets before feeding to Scikit-learn ML models.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Vibration analysis and predictive maintenance logs.
- **Finance:** Stock price manipulation, OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) dataframes.
- **Biomedical:** Patient record analysis, clinical trial statistics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Example 1: Loading and Inspecting a Real Dataset

**Problem:** Load a CSV file containing student marks and perform initial inspection.

**Step 1 — Import and Read:**
```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('students.csv')
```
*Explanation:* The `read_csv` function parses the file, uses the first row as column headers by default, and creates an integer `RangeIndex` (0, 1, 2, ...) for rows.

**Step 2 — Inspect First Few Rows:**
```python
print(df.head(5))
```
*Explanation:* `head(k)` returns the first $k$ rows. This is critical for verifying that parsing was correct (delimiters, headers, encoding).

**Step 3 — Inspect Structure:**
```python
print(df.shape)        # (rows, columns)
print(df.dtypes)       # Data type of each column
print(df.info())       # Summary: dtypes + non-null counts
print(df.describe())   # Statistics (mean, std, min, max) for numeric columns
```
*Explanation:* `describe()` computes:
$$\text{mean}(\mu) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$
$$\text{std}(\sigma) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Example 2: Boolean Indexing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Problem:** From a sales DataFrame, find all transactions where the amount exceeds ₹10,000 AND occurred in the "Kerala" region.

**Step 1 — Construct DataFrame:**
```python
data = {
    'OrderID': [101, 102, 103, 104, 105],
    'Region':  ['Kerala', 'Tamil Nadu', 'Kerala', 'Karnataka', 'Kerala'],
    'Amount':  [15000, 8000, 22000, 12000, 5000]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
```

**Step 2 — Construct the Boolean Mask (Condition 1):**
```python
mask_amount = df['Amount'] > 10000
```
This produces the Boolean Series:
$$\text{mask}_{\text{amount}} = [T, F, T, T, F]$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Boolean Mask (Condition 2):**
```python
mask_region = df['Region'] == 'Kerala'
```
This produces:
$$\text{mask}_{\text{region}} = [T, F, T, F, T]$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Logical AND (`&`):**
```python
final_mask = mask_amount & mask_region
```
Element-wise AND:
$$\text{final} = [T \wedge T,\ F \wedge F,\ T \wedge T,\ T \wedge F,\ F \wedge T] = [T, F, T, F, F]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Mask:**
```python
result = df[final_mask]
```
Pandas uses the Boolean Series as a filter, keeping only rows where `True`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Use `&`, `|`, `~` for element-wise logical operations. The Python keywords `and`, `or`, `not` will throw a `ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous` because they try to evaluate the **entire Series** as a single truth value.

## 3.3 Analytical Example 3: Vectorized Arithmetic vs. Python Loops

**Problem:** Compute the total price = quantity × unit_price for 1 million rows.

**Naive Python Loop Approach (Slow):**
```python
totals = []
for i in range(len(df)):
    totals.append(df.iloc[i]['qty'] * df.iloc[i]['price'])
df['total'] = totals
```
*Time Complexity:* $O(n)$ Python-level iterations → extremely slow (~30 seconds for 1M rows).

**Pandas Vectorized Approach (Fast):**
```python
df['total'] = df['qty'] * df['price']
```
*Time Complexity:* $O(n)$ but executed in optimized C code → typically **~0.05 seconds** for 1M rows.

**Why is it faster?** Pandas translates the operation into a single C-level loop over the underlying NumPy arrays, bypassing Python's interpreter overhead. The speedup factor is typically:
$$\text{Speedup} \approx \frac{T_{\text{Python loop}}}{T_{\text{vectorized}}} \approx 50 \times \text{ to } 500 \times$$

## 3.4 Analytical Example 4: `.loc[]` vs `.iloc[]` — The Label vs Position Distinction

**Setup:**
```python
df = pd.DataFrame({'Marks': [85, 90, 78]}, index=['A', 'B', 'C'])
```

| Operation | Syntax | Result | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Label-based (1st row) | `df.loc['A']` | Series: `Marks=85` | Looks up **label** 'A' |
| Position-based (1st row) | `df.iloc[0]` | Series: `Marks=85` | Looks up **index 0** |
| Slice by label (inclusive) | `df.loc['A':'B']` | Rows A and B | Both endpoints **included** |
| Slice by position (exclusive) | `df.iloc[0:2]` | Rows at index 0 and 1 | Endpoint **excluded** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Confusing `.loc[]` (label-based, inclusive slicing) with `.iloc[]` (integer-based, exclusive slicing). This single distinction accounts for ~40% of indexing-related mark losses.

## 3.5 Analytical Example 5: Missing Data Imputation

**Problem:** Replace missing `Age` values with the **median age** of the column.

**Step 1 — Detect Missing:**
```python
print(df['Age'].isnull().sum())    # Count of NaN in 'Age' column
```

**Step 2 — Compute Median:**
$$\text{median}(\tilde{x}) = \begin{cases} x_{(n+1)/2} & n \text{ odd} \\ \frac{x_{(n/2)} + x_{(n/2+1)}}{2} & n \text{ even} \end{cases}$$

```python
median_age = df['Age'].median()
```

**Step 3 — Impute:**
```python
df['Age'].fillna(median_age, inplace=True)
```

**Why median instead of mean?**
Median is **robust to outliers**. If one billionaire is in a dataset of 100 people's salaries, the mean will be heavily skewed, but the median will be unaffected. This is a standard KTU exam question.

## 3.6 Analytical Example 6: Adding a Computed Column using `apply()`

**Problem:** Categorize students into Pass/Fail based on a threshold.

```python
df['Result'] = df['Marks'].apply(lambda m: 'Pass' if m >= 50 else 'Fail')
```

**Logic Flow:**
$$\text{Result}_i = \begin{cases} \text{'Pass'} & \text{if } M_i \geq 50 \\ \text{'Fail'} & \text{if } M_i < 50 \end{cases}$$

The `apply()` function applies a Python function element-wise (or column-wise/row-wise depending on `axis`).

## 3.7 Analytical Example 7: Sorting with Multiple Keys

```python
df.sort_values(by=['Department', 'Salary'], ascending=[True, False], inplace=True)
```

*Explanation:* Sorts first by `Department` (A→Z), and within each department, sorts by `Salary` (high→low). This is equivalent to SQL's `ORDER BY Department ASC, Salary DESC`.

## 3.8 Analytical Example 8: `value_counts()` — Frequency Distribution

```python
df['Department'].value_counts()
```

Returns a Series with the **frequency** of each unique value, sorted in descending order by default.

For a column $C$ with $k$ unique values $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$:
$$\text{value\_counts}(c_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(C_i = c_j)$$
where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q: Define a Pandas DataFrame. List any four attributes of a DataFrame.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns)**, capable of holding heterogeneous data types (int, float, string, etc.).

Four important attributes of a DataFrame:
1. **`df.shape`** → Returns a tuple `(n_rows, n_columns)`.
2. **`df.dtypes`** → Returns the data type of each column.
3. **`df.columns`** → Returns the column index (column labels).
4. **`df.index`** → Returns the row index (row labels).
5. **`df.values`** → Returns the underlying NumPy array of values.
6. **`df.size`** → Total number of elements.

*(Any four correctly explained attributes earn full marks.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: Differentiate between `loc[]` and `iloc[]` in Pandas with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `loc[]` | `iloc[]` |
|---|---|---|
| Selection basis | **Label-based** (by row/column names) | **Integer position-based** (by 0-indexed positions) |
| Slicing behavior | **Both endpoints inclusive** | End-endpoint **exclusive** |
| Allowed inputs | Labels, label slices, Boolean arrays | Integers, integer slices, Boolean arrays |
| Error on missing label | Raises `KeyError` | Raises `IndexError` |

**Example:**
```python
df = pd.DataFrame({'Marks':[85,90,78]}, index=['A','B','C'])
df.loc['A':'B']     # Returns rows A and B  (both inclusive)
df.iloc[0:2]        # Returns rows at positions 0 and 1
```

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive DataFrame Operations*

**Q: Given the following Python code, explain each step and write the expected output. Also, demonstrate handling of missing data and Boolean filtering on the resulting DataFrame.**

```python
import pandas as pd
data = {
    'EmpID':   [101, 102, 103, 104, 105],
    'Name':    ['Anu', 'Ravi', 'Meera', 'John', 'Priya'],
    'Dept':    ['IT', 'HR', 'IT', 'Finance', 'HR'],

}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df.head(3))
print(df.shape)
print(df['Dept'].value_counts())
```

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explanation and Output**

**Step 1: Import**
```python
import pandas as pd
```
Imports the Pandas library with the conventional alias `pd`.

**Step 2: Construct Data**
A Python dictionary with four keys (`EmpID`, `Name`, `Dept`, `Salary`) and corresponding lists of values. Note that `Salary` contains `None` values, which Pandas will interpret as missing (`NaN`).

**Step 3: Create DataFrame**
```python

```
Creates a 5×4 DataFrame. Default integer row index (0–4) is assigned.

**Step 4: First Output — `df.head(3)`**
Returns the first 3 rows:

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 101 | Anu | IT | 50000.0 |
| 1 | 102 | Ravi | HR | NaN |
| 2 | 103 | Meera | IT | 60000.0 |

**Step 5: Second Output — `df.shape`**
Returns the tuple `(5, 4)` — meaning 5 rows and 4 columns.

**Step 6: Third Output — `df['Dept'].value_counts()`**
Counts frequency of each unique department:
- IT → 2
- HR → 2
- Finance → 1

Output:
```
IT        2
HR        2
Finance   1
Name: Dept, dtype: int64
```

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Missing Data + Boolean Filtering**

**Missing Data Handling:**
```python
# Detect missing values
print(df.isnull().sum())
# Output: EmpID 0, Name 0, Dept 0, Salary 2

# Fill missing Salary with mean

df['Salary'].fillna(mean_salary, inplace=True)
print(df)
```

**Boolean Filtering — Find IT employees earning above ₹55,000:**
```python

print(filtered)
```

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 103 | Meera | IT | 60000.0 |
