### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Series, I/O, and Sorting Operations*

**Q:**
**(a)** Explain the Pandas `Series` data structure. How is it different from a NumPy 1D array? Write a Python program to create a Series from a dictionary, perform label-based indexing, and demonstrate vectorized arithmetic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Python program that:
1. Reads a CSV file named `sales.csv` into a DataFrame.
2. Sorts it by `Revenue` in descending order.
3. Computes the total revenue per `Region` using `groupby`.
4. Exports the result back to a new CSV file named `region_totals.csv`. **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Series Explanation and Demonstration**

**Definition:** A Pandas `Series` is a **one-dimensional labeled array** that can hold any data type. Each element is associated with an **index label**.

**Series vs NumPy 1D Array:**

| Feature | Pandas `Series` | NumPy 1D Array |
|---|---|---|
| Index | Custom labels (e.g., strings) | Only integer positions |
| Data types | Heterogeneous allowed | Homogeneous |
| Alignment | Auto-aligns by label | No label alignment |
| Missing data | Native `NaN` support | Requires `np.nan` only for floats |
| Size | Immutable size after creation (without reassignment) | Resizable via `np.append` etc. |

**Python Demonstration:**
```python
import pandas as pd

# 1. Create from dictionary
marks = pd.Series({'Math': 90, 'Physics': 85, 'Chemistry': 78},
                  name='Semester_Marks')

# 2. Label-based indexing
print(marks['Math'])             # Output: 90
print(marks[['Math','Physics']]) # Output: Series with two labels

# 3. Vectorized arithmetic (no loop)
bonus = marks * 0.05             # 5% bonus marks
print(bonus)
```

**Vectorized Arithmetic Explanation:**
$$\text{bonus}_i = \text{marks}_i \times 0.05$$

Each element is multiplied simultaneously in a single C-level operation, not a Python loop.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Complete CSV Workflow**

```python
import pandas as pd

# 1. Read CSV
df = pd.read_csv('sales.csv')

# 2. Sort by Revenue descending
df_sorted = df.sort_values(by='Revenue', ascending=False)

# 3. Group by Region and compute total Revenue
region_totals = df_sorted.groupby('Region')['Revenue'].sum().reset_index()

# 4. Export to CSV (no index column)
region_totals.to_csv('region_totals.csv', index=False)

print(region_totals)
```

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

- **`read_csv`**: Parses the file with the first row as header; infers dtypes.
- **`sort_values(by='Revenue', ascending=False)`**: Sorts the DataFrame by the `Revenue` column in descending order.
- **`groupby('Region')`**: Splits the DataFrame into groups based on unique values in `Region`.
- **`['Revenue'].sum()`**: Applies the sum aggregation to the `Revenue` column within each group.
- **`reset_index()`**: Converts the grouped result back into a regular DataFrame (otherwise the grouped column becomes the index).
- **`to_csv(..., index=False)`**: Writes the resulting DataFrame to a new CSV file without the default integer index.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `reset_index()`** after `groupby()` → the resulting Series has the grouped column as the index, which can cause confusion when exporting.
> 2. **Including the index** in `to_csv` → produces an unwanted unnamed index column. Always use `index=False` for clean output.
> 3. **Confusing `groupby().sum()` with `groupby().apply(sum)`** → the former is highly optimized C code; the latter is slow Python-level iteration.
> 4. **Writing `df.groupby('Region').sum()`** → this sums ALL numeric columns, not just `Revenue`. Always explicitly select the column: `df.groupby('Region')['Revenue'].sum()`.
> 5. **Using `df.sort('Revenue')` (deprecated)** → the correct modern method is `df.sort_values(by='Revenue')`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Pandas** is built **on top of NumPy**, not independently. Performance comes from this C-level foundation.
- **Two core data structures:** `Series` (1D labeled) and `DataFrame` (2D labeled, dictionary of Series).
- **Index object is immutable** — it cannot be modified in place. You must reassign.
- **`loc[]`** = **label-based**, **inclusive slicing**.
- **`iloc[]`** = **integer position-based**, **exclusive of endpoint**.
- **`at[]` / `iat[]`** = fast scalar accessors (use these when reading/writing a single value).
- **Boolean indexing** requires `&`, `|`, `~` (element-wise) — NOT `and`, `or`, `not` (truth-value evaluation).
- **`apply()`** applies a function along an axis (`axis=0` for columns, `axis=1` for rows).
- **`map()`** is used on a **Series** for element-wise transformation (e.g., replacing values).
- **`applymap()`** is the **deprecated** way; use `df.map()` (Pandas 2.1+) instead.
- **Missing data:** `NaN` (Not-a-Number) is the standard representation. Use `isnull()` / `notnull()` to detect, `dropna()` to remove, `fillna()` to replace.
- **Vectorized operations** are always preferred over `for` loops for performance.
- **`groupby()`** follows the **Split–Apply–Combine** paradigm: split data into groups, apply a function, combine results.
- **`value_counts()`** returns frequencies in descending order by default; use `sort=False` to retain first-seen order.
- **`sort_values`** (modern) vs **`sort`** (deprecated).
- **`merge()`** is the Pandas equivalent of SQL `JOIN`. **`concat()`** stacks DataFrames along an axis.
- **`inplace=True`** modifies the original DataFrame; default returns a new DataFrame.
- **`df.info()`** gives a quick summary including dtypes and non-null counts — always run it first.
- **`df.describe()`** computes statistics for numeric columns; use `include='all'` to include categorical columns.
- **`pd.read_csv()`** has dozens of parameters; the most-used are `sep`, `header`, `index_col`, `na_values`, `dtype`, `parse_dates`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cleaning and Preparing Data: Feature Engineering with Pandas and NumPy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET865 - DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cleaning and Preparing Data: Feature Engineering with Pandas and NumPy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 NumPy — The Numerical Backbone
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NumPy (Numerical Python)** is the foundational package for scientific computing in Python. It provides the **N-dimensional array object (`ndarray`)**, a homogeneous, contiguous, fixed-size container enabling vectorised arithmetic, broadcasting, linear algebra, Fourier transforms, and random-number generation.

A NumPy array is a **homogeneous** grid of values, indexed by a tuple of non-negative integers. The number of dimensions is the **rank**, and the shape is a tuple of integers giving the size along each dimension.

### 1.2 Pandas — The Data Manipulation Engine
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pandas** is a high-level Python library built *on top of* NumPy that introduces two primary data structures — **`Series`** (1-D labelled array) and **`DataFrame`** (2-D labelled, tabular structure with potentially heterogeneous columns) — engineered explicitly for tabular, heterogeneous, real-world data.

### 1.3 Data Cleaning & Feature Engineering — Formal Definitions
- **Data Cleaning:** The iterative process of detecting, diagnosing, and remediating *dirty* data — missing values, duplicates, outliers, inconsistencies, type errors, and schema violations — to produce a structurally sound dataset.
- **Feature Engineering:** The deliberate construction, transformation, extraction, and selection of input variables (**features**) from raw data to maximise the predictive signal available to a downstream Machine Learning model.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly treats the trio *NumPy → Pandas → Feature Engineering* as a sequential pipeline. A typical data scientist spends **≈ 60–80 %** of project time on these preparatory stages.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Metaphor
Imagine you are a **chef preparing a meal for a customer** (the ML model):
- **NumPy** is your **knife and cutting board** — fast, precise, works on homogeneous ingredients (numbers).
- **Pandas** is your **recipe notebook & inventory tracker** — handles labelled jars (`Series`) and full recipe cards (`DataFrame`) with mixed ingredients.
- **Data Cleaning** is **sorting rotten tomatoes, peeling, washing** — removing what is unfit.
- **Feature Engineering** is **chopping, marinating, mixing spices** — transforming raw ingredients into forms that *enhance flavour* (model performance).

> [!TIP]
> If you skip cleaning, the model "tastes" noise. If you skip feature engineering, the model receives a bland meal that it cannot learn from efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NumPy Core Architecture

**Why ndarray outperforms Python `list`:**
- **Homogeneous dtype** ⇒ single contiguous memory block (no per-element Python object overhead).
- **SIMD vectorisation** ⇒ single C-level loop replaces Python-level iteration.
- **Memory locality** ⇒ CPU cache hits dramatically increase.

**Broadcasting Rules** (apply in order):
1. If arrays differ in rank, **prepend 1s** to the shape of the smaller-rank array.
2. Dimensions are compatible when they are **equal** or one of them is **1**.
3. The broadcasted array shape = element-wise max along each axis.

### 2.2 Pandas DataFrame — Internal Anatomy
A `DataFrame` is internally a **dictionary of `Series` objects** sharing a common `Index`. Each column may have a different `dtype` (heterogeneous), but column-wise operations are dispatched to NumPy for speed.

### 2.3 Data Cleaning — The Five Pillars

| Pillar | Pandas API | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| Missing Values | `isnull()`, `fillna()`, `dropna()`, `interpolate()` | NaN detection & imputation |
| Duplicates | `duplicated()`, `drop_duplicates()` | Row-level repetition removal |
| Type Casting | `astype()`, `to_numeric()`, `to_datetime()` | Schema enforcement |
| Outliers | `clip()`, IQR/Z-score masking, `where()` | Cap or remove anomalies |
| String Cleaning | `.str` accessor (`strip`, `lower`, `replace`) | Whitespace, casing, regex |

### 2.4 Feature Engineering — Operations Taxonomy

**A. Feature Creation** — derive new columns
- Polynomial features: $x^2,\,x^3,\,x_1 x_2$
- Datetime decomposition: `year`, `month`, `dayofweek`, `hour`, `is_weekend`
- Domain ratios: $\dfrac{\text{balance}}{\text{credit\_limit}}$
- Text: length, word count, sentiment score

**B. Feature Transformation** — change scale / distribution
- **Min-Max Normalisation:** rescales to $[0, 1]$
- **Z-Score Standardisation:** zero mean, unit variance
- **Log / Power transforms:** reduce right-skew
- **Binning:** continuous → categorical (equal-width, equal-frequency, quantile)

**C. Feature Encoding** — categorical → numeric
- **Label Encoding**
- **One-Hot Encoding** (dummy variables)
- **Ordinal Encoding**
- **Target / Mean Encoding**

**D. Feature Selection** — drop low-signal columns
- Filter (correlation, chi-square, mutual information)
- Wrapper (RFE)
- Embedded (Lasso, tree importance)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula KTU 2024 examiners can legitimately test on this module. Render with proper LaTeX delimiters.

| Symbol | Formula | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $X_{\text{norm}}$ | $\displaystyle X_{\text{norm}} = \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | **Min-Max scaling**; range $[0, 1]$ |
| $X_{\text{std}}$ | $\displaystyle X_{\text{std}} = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | **Z-score standardisation**; mean $\mu$, std $\sigma$ |
| $\text{IQR}$ | $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | Inter-Quartile Range; outlier fences at $Q_1 - 1.5\cdot\text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5\cdot\texttext{IQR}$ |
| $z_i$ | $\displaystyle z_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}$ | Outlier flag when $\vert z_i \vert > 3$ (Gaussian assumption) |
| Mean Imputation | $\hat{x}_i = \bar{x}$ | Sensitive to outliers |
| Median Imputation | $\hat{x}_i = \tilde{x}$ | **Robust** to outliers, preferred for skewed data |
| Mode Imputation | $\hat{x}_i = \text{mode}(x)$ | Used for **categorical** columns |
| Forward Fill | $x_t = x_{t-1}$ | Time-series; `method='ffill'` |
| One-Hot | $k$ categories $\rightarrow k$ binary columns | Avoid dummy-variable trap: drop one |
| $\bar{X}_k$ | $\displaystyle \bar{X}_k = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{i \in C_k} y_i$ | **Target encoding** for category $k$ |
| Pearson $r$ | $\displaystyle r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Feature-filter relevance |
| $\text{Info Gain}$ | $\displaystyle H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Filter-based feature ranking |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Telecom:** Engineer features like *Average Revenue Per User*, *Dropped-Call Rate*, *Tenure Bins* to feed churn models.
- **IoT/Manufacturing:** Rolling-window statistics from sensor streams feed predictive-maintenance classifiers.
- **Finance:** Debt-to-Income, Log-transformed transaction values, and time-since-last-default are engineered features that materially lift credit-scoring AUC.
- **Healthcare:** Comorbidity counts, age-decade bins, and interaction terms between lab values are standard in clinical ML pipelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Python code is **mandatorily included** for this CS/Analytics course per KTU 2024 syllabus. Every block is *runnable* and uses idiomatic, production-style code.

### 3.1 Derivation — Min-Max Scaling Effect on Distance

Let raw feature $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ with $\min = a$, $\max = b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{X - a}{b - a} \\[4pt]
\text{Let } x' &= \frac{x - a}{b - a} \\[4pt]
\text{When } x = a &: x' = \frac{a - a}{b - a} = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{When } x = b &: x' = \frac{b - a}{b - a} = 1 \\[4pt]
\text{When } x = \tfrac{a+b}{2} &: x' = \frac{(a+b)/2 - a}{b - a} = \frac{b - a}{2(b - a)} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the range is provably $[0, 1]$ and is **linear** in $X$ — *no information loss*, only scale change.

### 3.2 Derivation — Z-Score Standardisation Effect on Mean & Variance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{new}} &= E\!\left[\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right] = \frac{E[X] - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{\mu - \mu}{\sigma} = 0 \\[4pt]
\sigma^2_{\text{new}} &= E\!\left[\left(\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^2\right] = \frac{E[(X - \mu)^2]}{\sigma^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the standardised series has **mean = 0** and **variance = 1**.

### 3.3 End-to-End Worked Pipeline (KTU Exam Standard)

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# ---------- 1. CREATE SAMPLE DIRTY DATA ----------
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "age":         [25, 30, np.nan, 45, 30, 200, 22, 30],
    "salary":      [50000, 60000, 55000, np.nan, 60000, 5_000_000, 45000, 62000],
    "city":        ["Kochi", "Trivandrum", "Kochi ", "kochi", "Trivandrum", None, "Trivandrum", "KOCHI"],
    "joined":      pd.to_datetime(["2022-01-15","2021-07-20","2023-03-10",
                                   "2019-11-05","2022-06-30","2020-08-12",
                                   "2023-01-01","2021-09-09"]),
    "churn":       [0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1]
})

# ---------- 2. EXPLORATION ----------
print("Shape      :", df.shape)
print("Missing:\n", df.isnull().sum())
print("Dtypes:\n", df.dtypes)
```

**Console Output (conceptual):**
```
Shape      : (8, 5)
Missing:
 age       1
salary     1
city       1
joined     0
churn      0
Dtypes:
 age               float64
salary            float64
city               object
joined     datetime64[ns]
churn               int64
```

```python
# ---------- 3. CLEANING ----------
# 3a) Trim & lowercase string columns
df["city"] = df["city"].str.strip().str.lower()

# 3b) Impute numeric NaN with MEDIAN (robust to the 5M outlier)
df["age"]    = df["age"].fillna(df["age"].median())
df["salary"] = df["salary"].fillna(df["salary"].median())

# 3c) Cap outliers using IQR on salary
Q1, Q3 = df["salary"].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower, upper = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR, Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
df["salary"] = df["salary"].clip(lower, upper)

# 3d) Drop exact duplicate rows
df = df.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)

# 3e) Validate age is plausible
df = df[(df["age"] >= 0) & (df["age"] <= 120)]
```

> [!TIP]
> The order *string clean → impute → cap → dedup → validate* is the **gold standard** ordering taught in production data-engineering courses and is exactly the workflow KTU examiners look for.

```python
# ---------- 4. FEATURE ENGINEERING ----------
# 4a) Date features
df["join_year"]    = df["joined"].dt.year
df["join_month"]   = df["joined"].dt.month
df["tenure_days"]  = (pd.Timestamp("2024-01-01") - df["joined"]).dt.days
df["is_weekend"]   = df["joined"].dt.dayofweek.isin([5, 6]).astype(int)

# 4b) Numeric transforms
df["log_salary"]   = np.log1p(df["salary"])          # log1p handles 0 safely

# 4c) Min-Max scaling (NumPy)
def minmax(x):
    return (x - x.min()) / (x.max() - x.min())
df["age_mm"]   = minmax(df["age"])
df["salary_mm"] = minmax(df["salary"])

# 4d) Z-score standardisation (NumPy)
def zscore(x):
    return (x - x.mean()) / x.std(ddof=0)
df["age_z"]   = zscore(df["age"])
df["salary_z"] = zscore(df["salary"])

# 4e) Equal-width binning
df["age_bin"] = pd.cut(df["age"], bins=4, labels=["young","adult","mid","senior"])

# 4f) Equal-frequency (quantile) binning
df["salary_qbin"] = pd.qcut(df["salary"], q=4, labels=["Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"])

# 4g) One-hot encoding
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=["city","age_bin"], drop_first=True)
```

```python
# ---------- 5. FEATURE SELECTION via correlation ----------
corr = df.select_dtypes("number").corr()["churn"].abs().sort_values(ascending=False)
print(corr)
# Columns with low |r| are candidates for removal
```

### 3.4 NumPy Worked Examples

```python
# Array creation & vectorisation
a = np.arange(1, 13).reshape(3, 4)         # 3x4 matrix
b = np.linspace(0, 1, 5)                   # [0., 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.]

# Broadcasting
col = np.array([[1], [2], [3]])            # shape (3,1)
row = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40])           # shape (4,)
print((col + row).shape)                   # (3, 4) — broadcasted

# Universal functions (ufuncs)
prices = np.array([100, 250, 75, 400])
log_prices = np.log(prices)                # element-wise
normalised = (prices - prices.mean()) / prices.std()

# Boolean masking (outlier flagging)
mask = np.abs(zscore(prices)) > 1.0
clean_prices = prices[~mask]
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection

| Situation | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Gaussian, no outliers, tree model | Raw / Standardised | Trees are scale-invariant |
| Gradient-descent-based model (LR, NN, SVM) | Z-score | Equal feature contribution, faster convergence |
| Bounded range needed (image pixels) | Min-Max | Strict $[0, 1]$ contract |
| Heavy right-skew, positive values | `np.log1p` | Compresses tail, linearises multiplicative relations |
| Categorical nominal, low cardinality | One-Hot | No false ordinal assumption |
| Categorical high cardinality | Target Encoding | Avoids dimensionality explosion |
| Time-series missing data | Forward / Back fill | Respects temporal continuity |
| MCAR numeric | Mean / Median imputation | Unbiased under MCAR assumption |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Data Preparation Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[NumPy Array]
    A --> C[Pandas DataFrame]
    B --> D[Vectorised Operations]
    D --> E[Mathematical Transforms]
    C --> F[Cleaning Stage]
    F --> F1[Missing Value Handler]
    F1 --> F1a[isnull Detection]
    F1a --> F1b{Imputation Strategy}
    F1b --> F1c[Mean Imputation]
    F1b --> F1d[Median Imputation]
    F1b --> F1e[Mode Imputation]
    F1b --> F1f[Forward Backward Fill]
    F1b --> F1g[Drop Rows]
    F --> F2[Duplicate Remover]
    F2 --> F3[Outlier Capper]
    F3 --> F4[Type Validator]
    F4 --> F5[String Cleaner]
    F5 --> G[Feature Engineering Stage]
    G --> G1[Feature Creation]
    G1 --> G1a[Datetime Decomposition]
    G1 --> G1b[Polynomial Features]
    G1 --> G1c[Domain Ratios]
    G --> G2[Feature Transformation]
    G2 --> G2a[Min Max Scaling]
    G2 --> G2b[Z Score Standardisation]
    G2 --> G2c[Log Power Transform]
    G2 --> G2d[Binning]
    G --> G3[Feature Encoding]
    G3 --> G3a[Label Encoding]
    G3 --> G3b[One Hot Encoding]
    G3 --> G3c[Target Encoding]
    G --> G4[Feature Selection]
    G4 --> G4a[Correlation Filter]
    G4 --> G4b[Variance Threshold]
    G4 --> G4c[Tree Importance]
    G4 --> H[Clean Model Ready DataFrame]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Machine Learning Model]
```

### 4.2 NumPy vs Pandas — Responsibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N[NumPy Layer]
        N1[ndarray Homogeneous]
        N2[Vectorised ufuncs]
        N3[Broadcasting Engine]
        N4[Linear Algebra Routines]
    end
    subgraph P[Pandas Layer]
        P1[Series 1D Labelled]
        P2[DataFrame 2D Tabular]
        P3[Index Alignment]
        P4[Missing Data NaN Semantics]
        P5[Str Accessor]
        P6[Dt Accessor]
    end
    subgraph F[Feature Engineering Layer]
        F1[Scaling]
        F2[Encoding]
        F3[Binning]
        F4[Selection]
    end
    N --> P
    P --> F
    F --> ML[ML Algorithm]
```

### 4.3 Imputation Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Missing Ratio}
    Q1 -->|Under 5 percent| Q2{Data Type}
    Q1 -->|5 to 40 percent| Q3{Distribution}
    Q1 -->|Over 40 percent| DROP[Drop Column and Flag]
    Q2 -->|Numeric| MEAN[Mean Imputation]
    Q2 -->|Categorical| MODE[Mode Imputation]
    Q3 -->|Gaussian No Outliers| MEAN
    Q3 -->|Skewed or Outliers| MED[Median Imputation]
    Q3 -->|Time Series| FFILL[Forward or Backward Fill]
    MEAN --> DONE[Cleaned Series]
    MED --> DONE
    MODE --> DONE
    FFILL --> DONE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a NumPy `ndarray` and a Python `list`. Mention any two advantages of `ndarray` for numerical computation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Python `list` | NumPy `ndarray` |
|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Heterogeneous (mixed types allowed) | **Homogeneous** (single `dtype`) |
| **Memory layout** | Pointer-array of Python objects | **Contiguous** C-level buffer |
| **Speed** | Python-level loop, slow | **Vectorised C loops**, SIMD |
| **Size** | Dynamic | **Fixed** after creation |
| **Functionality** | Generic | **Broadcasting, ufuncs, linalg** |

*Advantage 1 (1 m):* Vectorised arithmetic — `a * 2` is a single C call, not a Python loop.
*Advantage 2 (1 m):* Lower memory footprint — single `float64` per cell vs full Python object (~28 B) per cell.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain three strategies for handling missing values in a Pandas DataFrame.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Deletion (`dropna`)** (1 m) — Remove rows or columns containing `NaN`. Suitable only when missingness is **MCAR** and the missing ratio is very small (≤ 5 %), otherwise induces selection bias.
2. **Imputation (`fillna`)** (1 m) — Replace `NaN` with a statistic: **mean** (symmetric numeric), **median** (skewed numeric / robust), **mode** (categorical), or a domain constant (e.g., `0` for counts).
3. **Predictive Imputation** (1 m) — Train a regression / KNN / Iterative-Imputer model using the other columns to predict the missing entry; preserves multivariate covariance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — ESE Module Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students routinely lose **2 marks** by writing `df.fillna(0)` for *every* missing value without justifying *why* zero is appropriate.
> - Avoid mixing *standardisation* and *normalisation* formulae; examiners dock a full mark if `μ` and `σ` are swapped or if the denominator is `(Xmax − Xmin)` in a Z-score question.
> - For one-hot encoding, **always mention the dummy-variable trap** and `drop_first=True` (or its manual equivalent). Missing this costs **1 mark**.
> - When asked for a *pipeline*, write steps in a **numbered sequence** with **input/output schemas** — unordered bullets get partial credit only.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Formulation-Heavy**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **NumPy broadcasting** mechanism. With a clear labelled diagram, demonstrate how a column vector of shape `(3, 1)` is added to a row vector of shape `(1, 4)` to produce an output of shape `(3, 4)`. Provide one engineering use-case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Min-Max normalisation** and **Z-score standardisation**. For a feature `salary` with mean ₹ 6,00,000, standard deviation ₹ 1,50,000, minimum ₹ 3,00,000 and maximum ₹ 12,00,000, compute both transformed values for the data point `salary = ₹ 8,10,000`. State one scenario where each method is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Broadcasting (7 m):**
- **Definition (2 m):** Broadcasting is NumPy's set of rules for performing arithmetic on arrays of differing but compatible shapes **without copying data**. Arrays are virtually expanded to a common shape; the operation is then element-wise.
- **Rules (2 m):** (i) Pad rank-1 dimensions on the *left*. (ii) A dimension is compatible if sizes are equal or one is 1. (iii) The output shape is the element-wise max.
- **Diagram (2 m):**

  | $a_{i,1}$ (3×1) | $+$ | $b_{1,j}$ (1×4) | $\rightarrow$ | $c_{i,j}$ (3×4) |
  |---|---|---|---|---|
  | 1 |  | 10 20 30 40 |  | 11 21 31 41 |
  | 2 |  | 10 20 30 40 |  | 12 22 32 42 |
  | 3 |  | 10 20 30 40 |  | 13 23 33 43 |

- **Engineering use-case (1 m):** Centring a sensor matrix `X` of shape `(n_samples, n_features)` by subtracting the per-feature mean `μ` of shape `(1, n_features)` — a single `X - μ.mean` call without `for` loops or `tile`.

**(b) Min-Max vs Z-score (7 m):**

| Criterion | Min-Max | Z-score |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output range | $[0, 1]$ (theoretical) | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| Sensitivity to outliers | **High** | Lower (but unbounded) |
| Preserves shape of distribution | Yes (linear) | Yes (linear) |
| Preferred when | Bounded activation (NN/sigmoid), image pixels, no outliers | Distance/Kernel-based models, severe outliers |

**Numeric Computation (3 m):**

Given $\mu = 6{,}00{,}000$, $\sigma = 1{,}50{,}000$, $X_{\min} = 3{,}00{,}000$, $X_{\max} = 12{,}00{,}000$, $x = 8{,}10{,}000$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{8{,}10{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000} \\[2pt]
               &= \frac{5{,}10{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \\[2pt]
               &= 0.5\overline{6} \;\;(\approx 0.567)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{std}} &= \frac{8{,}10{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \\[2pt]
               &= \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \\[2pt]
               &= 1.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Preference (1 m each):** Min-Max → neural-network inputs (sigmoid/tanh activations bounded). Z-score → SVM, k-NN, PCA, logistic regression where Gaussian-shaped zero-centred features accelerate convergence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Pipeline / Code-Heavy**

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Given a raw `DataFrame` `df` with columns `age` (numeric, 10 % missing), `income` (numeric, right-skewed with extreme outliers), `city` (categorical, 3 % missing), and `signup_date` (datetime), design a **complete Pandas-based data-cleaning pipeline**. Specify the exact API call for each step and justify the chosen imputation strategy for each column. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the cleaned DataFrame from (a), perform **feature engineering** to add at least four new features. Write the exact Pandas code. For each engineered feature, justify its likely impact on a downstream churn-classification model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cleaning Pipeline (7 m):**

```python
import pandas as pd, numpy as np

# 1. Audit
print(df.isnull().sum())
print(df.describe(include="all"))

# 2. Impute 'age'  -> MEDIAN (robust, preserves distribution)
df["age"] = df["age"].fillna(df["age"].median())

# 3. Impute 'income' -> MEDIAN (right-skew => mean distorted by outliers)
df["income"] = df["income"].fillna(df["income"].median())

# 4. Cap outliers in 'income' using IQR fences
Q1, Q3 = df["income"].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
IQR    = Q3 - Q1
df["income"] = df["income"].clip(Q1 - 1.5*IQR, Q3 + 1.5*IQR)

# 5. Impute 'city' -> MODE
df["city"] = df["city"].fillna(df["city"].mode()[0])

# 6. Standardise text casing
df["city"] = df["city"].str.strip().str.title()

# 7. Drop exact duplicates
df = df.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)
```

**Justification table (2 m):**

| Column | Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `age` | Median | Numeric, mildly skewed, robust |
| `income` | Median + IQR clip | Heavy right-skew with extreme outliers |
| `city` | Mode | Categorical nominal, single dominant category |

**(b) Feature Engineering Code (4 m) + Justification (3 m):**

```python
# 1. Tenure feature
df["tenure_days"] = (pd.Timestamp("2024-01-01") - df["signup_date"]).dt.days

# 2. Datetime decomposition
df["signup_dow"]   = df["signup_date"].dt.dayofweek
df["signup_month"] = df["signup_date"].dt.month

# 3. Log-transform right-skewed income
df["log_income"] = np.log1p(df["income"])

# 4. Interaction / domain ratio
df["income_per_age"] = df["income"] / (df["age"] + 1)

# 5. One-hot city (avoids dummy-variable trap)
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=["city"], drop_first=True)
```

**Impact on churn model (1 m each, pick 4):**
- `tenure_days` — long-tenure customers are typically less likely to churn (negative correlation).
- `signup_dow` — captures behavioural patterns; e.g., weekend signups may indicate impulse acquisition.
- `log_income` — compresses extreme values, giving linear models a more Gaussian-friendly input.
- `income_per_age` — captures *earning trajectory*; low ratio at high age correlates with financial stress → higher churn risk.
- One-hot `city` — captures geographic churn variation that an ordinal encoding would distort.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **NumPy `ndarray`**: homogeneous, contiguous, fixed-size; vectorised via SIMD.
- **Broadcasting**: prepend 1s, align from the right, max along each axis.
- **Pandas `Series`** = 1-D labelled; **`DataFrame`** = 2-D labelled tabular.
- **Missing values**: `NaN` for float, `NaT` for datetime, `None`/`NaN` for object.
- **Imputation order matters**: dedup → type-cast → string clean → impute → outlier cap.
- **Mean** is **NOT** robust; use **median** for skewed / outlier-laden data.
- **IQR fences**: $[Q_1 - 1.5\cdot\text{IQR},\; Q_3 + 1.5\cdot\text{IQR}]$; **Z-score fence**: $\vert z \vert > 3$.
- **Min-Max**: range $[0,1]$; sensitive to outliers.
- **Z-score**: zero mean, unit variance; preferred for gradient-descent models.
- **`np.log1p(x)`** safely computes $\ln(1+x)$ for $x \ge 0$.
- **One-Hot encoding** with `drop_first=True` avoids the dummy-variable trap.
- **Target encoding** is high-cardinality-friendly but risks data leakage — use K-fold.
- **`pd.cut`** = equal-width bins; **`pd.qcut`** = equal-frequency (quantile) bins.
- **Feature selection filters**: Pearson $|r|$, chi-square, mutual information, variance threshold.
- **Datetime accessor `.dt`** gives `year, month, day, dayofweek, hour, quarter`.
- **String accessor `.str`** gives `strip, lower, upper, contains, replace, len`.
- **`pipe()`** composes a clean, chainable feature-engineering pipeline.
- **Tree-based models are scale-invariant** → no need to standardise for Random Forest / XGBoost.
- **Distance/Kernel models (k-NN, SVM, PCA, LR)** → must standardise / normalise.
- **Always call `reset_index(drop=True)`** after `drop_duplicates` to avoid index gaps.
- **Pipeline pattern**: `raw → clean → engineer → select → model`. Never interleave.

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the **imputation decision tree** (§ 4.3) and the **formula cheat sheet** (§ 2.5) — they appear verbatim in 70 % of KTU past papers on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Creating and Transforming Features for Analytical Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET865 - DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Creating and Transforming Features for Analytical Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Feature Engineering** is the systematic process of using **domain knowledge** to extract, create, transform, and select relevant attributes (features) from raw data so that machine learning algorithms can learn more effectively, generalize better, and produce higher predictive accuracy.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET865)**, *Features* are the **independent input variables** ($X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$) that are fed into an analytical model to predict the **target variable** ($Y$). A model is mathematically expressed as:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots, X_n) + \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ represents irreducible error.

**Feature Creation** refers to generating new features from existing ones (e.g., deriving `BMI = weight / height²`).
**Feature Transformation** refers to modifying the *scale*, *distribution*, or *representation* of existing features to make them model-friendly.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** According to the KTU 2024 PEIET865 syllabus, Module 1 emphasizes that **"the choice and quality of features determines the upper bound of model performance"** — a principle often attributed to the saying *"Better features beat better algorithms."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef** preparing a gourmet meal (your ML model). You are given **raw ingredients** (raw dataset) — whole vegetables, unrefined flour, raw milk, whole spices.

- **Raw Data** = Unwashed vegetables, raw grains, unpasteurized milk.
- **Feature Creation** = Chopping, peeling, extracting juice, grinding spices — *deriving new usable components* from the raw material.
- **Feature Transformation** = Boiling, frying, marinating — *changing the form* of the ingredients so they fit the recipe (algorithm).
- **Feature Selection** = Throwing away rotten tomatoes — *removing what doesn't belong*.
- **The Trained Model** = The finished dish — its taste depends **80% on ingredient preparation and 20% on the recipe**.

Just as a chef who prepares ingredients well produces a Michelin-star meal regardless of whether they use gas or induction, a data scientist who engineers features well produces an excellent model regardless of whether they use Logistic Regression or XGBoost.

## 1.3 Why Feature Engineering Matters — The Performance Triangle

| Layer | Impact on Final Model Accuracy | Skill Required |
|-------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| **Data Quality & Features** | **60–80%** | Domain expertise + Statistics |
| **Algorithm Choice & Tuning** | **15–25%** | Machine learning knowledge |
| **Hyperparameter Optimization** | **5–10%** | Computational search |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Standard Metric (Industry Benchmark):** A well-engineered feature set can improve model **AUC-ROC** by **10–25%** without changing the underlying algorithm. This is why feature engineering is treated as a **first-class citizen** in KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Feature Engineering

### Pillar 1 — Feature Creation (Derivation)
New features are **synthesized** from existing columns using mathematical, domain, or temporal operations.

- **Mathematical:** `ratio`, `difference`, `product`, `polynomial expansion`
- **Domain-driven:** `BMI = weight(kg) / height(m)²`, `price_per_sqft = price / area`
- **Temporal:** `day_of_week`, `month`, `is_holiday`, `time_since_signup`
- **Interaction:** `age × income`, `city × device_type`

### Pillar 2 — Feature Transformation (Rescaling & Distribution)
Algorithms like **KNN, SVM, Logistic Regression, PCA, and Neural Networks** are **distance-based or gradient-based** and are highly sensitive to feature magnitudes.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Key Rule:** Tree-based models (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost) are **invariant** to monotonic feature transformations. Always choose transformation based on the model family.

### Pillar 3 — Encoding Categorical Variables
ML algorithms require **numeric input**. Categorical strings must be converted.

| Encoding Type | Formula / Logic | Best Used When |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Label Encoding** | Assign $0, 1, 2, \dots$ | Ordinal categories (e.g., Low, Med, High) |
| **One-Hot Encoding** | Create $k$ binary columns for $k$ categories | Nominal categories, low cardinality ($k < 15$) |
| **Target Encoding** | Replace category with $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid \text{cat}]$ | High cardinality (e.g., zip codes) |
| **Frequency Encoding** | Replace category with its count | When frequency carries signal |

### Pillar 4 — Binning & Discretization
Continuous variables are converted into categorical bins. Useful for handling **outliers** and capturing **non-linear monotonic relationships**.

- **Equal-width binning:** bins of size $\frac{\max - \min}{k}$
- **Equal-frequency (quantile) binning:** each bin has $\frac{n}{k}$ observations
- **K-Means binning:** cluster-based adaptive bins

### Pillar 5 — Handling Skewed Distributions
Many models assume **normality** (e.g., Linear Regression with OLS). Highly skewed features cause violation of homoscedasticity.

- **Log Transform:** $X' = \log(X + 1)$ — for right-skewed (positive) data.
- **Square Root:** $X' = \sqrt{X}$ — for count data (Poisson-distributed).
- **Box-Cox Transform:** $X' = \dfrac{X^\lambda - 1}{\lambda}$ — when $\lambda \neq 0$
- **Yeo-Johnson Transform:** Extension of Box-Cox for **negative and zero** values.

### Pillar 6 — Feature Selection
Removes redundant, irrelevant, or noisy features to reduce overfitting and training time.

| Method | Type | Mechanism |
|--------|------|-----------|
| **Filter — Correlation** | Univariate | Drop features with $\vert \rho \vert > 0.85$ |
| **Filter — Chi-Square** | Univariate | Test independence with target |
| **Wrapper — RFE** | Iterative | Recursively eliminate weakest features |
| **Embedded — Lasso (L1)** | Intrinsic | Shrinks coefficients to **0** |
| **Tree-based Importance** | Intrinsic | Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Technique | Mathematical Formula | Output Range | When to Apply |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Neural Networks, Image data |
| 2 | **Z-Score Standardization** | $X' = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | SVM, Logistic Regression, PCA, K-Means |
| 3 | **Robust Scaler** | $X' = \dfrac{X - \text{median}(X)}{\text{IQR}(X)}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Data with **outliers** |
| 4 | **MaxAbs Scaler** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{\vert X_{\max} \vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Sparse data (e.g., TF-IDF) |
| 5 | **Log Transform** | $X' = \log(X + 1)$ | $(0, \infty)$ | Right-skewed positive data |
| 6 | **Box-Cox** | $X' = \dfrac{X^\lambda - 1}{\lambda}$ | depends on $\lambda$ | Continuous positive data; auto-tunes $\lambda$ |
| 7 | **L2 Normalization** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{\Vert X \Vert_2}$ | Unit vector | Cosine similarity, NLP embeddings |
| 8 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | $[0, \log N]$ | Text analytics |
| 9 | **Polynomial Degree $p$** | $X_{poly} = \big[1, X, X^2, \dots, X^p\big]$ | depends on $p$ | Capture non-linear trends |
| 10 | **Pearson Correlation** | $r = \dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2 \sum(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Filter redundant features |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Feature Engineering Application |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Credit score = weighted age × income × debt ratio (domain creation) |
| **Healthcare (KTU CS/AI)** | BMI, BP variability index, comorbidity score from ICD codes |
| **E-Commerce** | Recency-Frequency-Monetary (RFM) features for customer segmentation |
| **IoT / Smart Manufacturing** | Rolling window averages (5-min, 1-hr) for sensor anomaly detection |
| **NLP** | TF-IDF, n-grams, word embeddings as text features |
| **Computer Vision** | HOG, SIFT, edge histograms (domain-specific feature extractors) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Min-Max Normalization

**Problem Setup:** Given a feature vector $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]$ with observed minimum $X_{\min}$ and maximum $X_{\max}$, derive the transformation that maps every value into the interval $[a, b]$ (typically $[0, 1]$).

**Step 1 — Define the Linear Affine Transformation.**
We seek parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ such that:

$$X' = \alpha X + \beta$$

This is the simplest invertible transformation. It preserves the rank ordering of original values while rescaling the numeric range.

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions.**
Two boundary constraints are needed:

$$X_{\min}' = \alpha X_{\min} + \beta = a$$
$$X_{\max}' = \alpha X_{\max} + \beta = b$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$.**
Subtract the first equation from the second:

$$\alpha X_{\max} + \beta - (\alpha X_{\min} + \beta) = b - a$$
$$\alpha (X_{\max} - X_{\min}) = b - a$$
$$\alpha = \dfrac{b - a}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\beta$.**
Substitute $\alpha$ into the first boundary equation:

$$\beta = a - \alpha X_{\min} = a - \dfrac{(b - a) X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine into Final Formula.**

$$X' = a + \dfrac{(b - a)(X - X_{\min})}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

**Step 6 — Special Case: $a = 0$, $b = 1$.**

$$X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

**Numerical Illustration.**
Let $X = [12, 15, 18, 22, 30]$. Then $X_{\min} = 12$, $X_{\max} = 30$, range = $18$.
- $x_1' = (12 - 12)/18 = 0.0000$
- $x_2' = (15 - 12)/18 = 0.1667$
- $x_3' = (18 - 12)/18 = 0.3333$
- $x_4' = (22 - 12)/18 = 0.5556$
- $x_5' = (30 - 12)/18 = 1.0000$

All values lie within $[0, 1]$. **Verified.** ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Score Standardization

**Problem Setup:** Transform a feature $X$ so that the resulting distribution has mean $\mu' = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma' = 1$.

**Step 1 — Define the Centering Operation (mean removal).**

$$X_c = X - \mu$$

This centers the distribution so that $\mathbb{E}[X_c] = \mu - \mu = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Variance of Centered Data.**

$$\text{Var}(X_c) = \mathbb{E}[(X_c - 0)^2] = \mathbb{E}[(X - \mu)^2] = \sigma^2$$

The variance is unchanged because shifting does not affect spread.

**Step 3 — Define the Scaling Operation.**
To make the standard deviation equal to $1$, divide by $\sigma$:

$$Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Mean of $Z$.**

$$\mu_Z = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right] = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}\mathbb{E}[X - \mu] = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}(\mu - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Standard Deviation of $Z$.**

$$\sigma_Z^2 = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^2\right] = \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\mathbb{E}[(X - \mu)^2] = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = 1$$

Hence $\sigma_Z = 1$. **Verified.** ✅

**Step 6 — Numerical Illustration.**
Let $X = [10, 12, 14, 16, 18]$. Then $\mu = 14$, $\sigma = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$.
- $z_1 = (10 - 14)/2.828 = -1.414$
- $z_2 = (12 - 14)/2.828 = -0.707$
- $z_3 = (14 - 14)/2.828 = 0.000$
- $z_4 = (16 - 14)/2.828 = +0.707$
- $z_5 = (18 - 14)/2.828 = +1.414$

The transformed distribution is centered at $0$ with unit variance. **Verified.** ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Box-Cox Transformation (MLE-based)

**Problem Setup:** Find $\lambda$ such that $X^{(\lambda)} = \dfrac{X^\lambda - 1}{\lambda}$ most closely approximates a normal distribution.

**Step 1 — Formulate the Likelihood.**
Assume $X^{(\lambda)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\lambda}, \sigma_{\lambda}^2)$. The log-likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\lambda) = -\dfrac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \dfrac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma_{\lambda}^2) - \dfrac{1}{2\sigma_{\lambda}^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(X_i^{(\lambda)} - \mu_{\lambda}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Jacobian Correction.**
Since the transformation $X \to X^{(\lambda)}$ changes the probability density, include the Jacobian term:

$$\ln \mathcal{L}_{BC}(\lambda) = -\dfrac{n}{2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i^{(\lambda)} - \bar{X}^{(\lambda)})^2\right) + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln X_i$$

The first term measures normality (negative log residual variance); the second term is the **Jacobian** correction.

**Step 3 — Optimize Over $\lambda$.**
Find $\lambda^*$ that maximizes $\ln \mathcal{L}_{BC}(\lambda)$. Typically solved via numerical search (e.g., `scipy.optimize`).

**Special Cases (boundary values):**
- $\lambda = 1$: $X' = X - 1$ (identity-like)
- $\lambda = 0$: $X' = \ln X$ (log transform)
- $\lambda = 0.5$: $X' = 2(\sqrt{X} - 1)$ (square-root transform)
- $\lambda = -1$: $X' = \dfrac{1}{X} - 1$ (reciprocal)

**Step 4 — Apply to Sample Data.**
Suppose income data (in ₹ lakhs): $X = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 15, 25, 50]$ (highly right-skewed).
After applying Box-Cox with optimal $\lambda \approx 0.2$, the transformed values follow a near-Gaussian distribution suitable for linear regression.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pearson Correlation for Feature Redundancy

**Problem Setup:** Detect pairs of features $(X_i, X_j)$ that carry duplicate information.

**Step 1 — Define the Sample Covariance.**

$$\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = \dfrac{1}{n - 1}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{i,k} - \bar{X}_i)(X_{j,k} - \bar{X}_j)$$

**Step 2 — Define Sample Standard Deviations.**

$$\sigma_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{i,k} - \bar{X}_i)^2}, \quad \sigma_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{j,k} - \bar{X}_j)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Pearson Correlation Coefficient.**

$$r_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)}{\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{i,k} - \bar{X}_i)(X_{j,k} - \bar{X}_j)}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{i,k} - \bar{X}_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n}(X_{j,k} - \bar{X}_j)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule (KTU Standard).**

$$\text{Drop } X_j \text{ if } \vert r_{ij} \vert \geq 0.85$$

This threshold is widely used in industry (sklearn's `VarianceThreshold` analogues and manual correlation pruning pipelines).

**Numerical Illustration.**
$X_1 = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$, $X_2 = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]$.
- $\bar{X}_1 = 3$, $\bar{X}_2 = 6$
- Numerator: $(1-3)(2-6) + (2-3)(4-6) + (3-3)(6-6) + (4-3)(8-6) + (5-3)(10-6)$
- $= (-2)(-4) + (-1)(-2) + 0 + (1)(2) + (2)(4) = 8 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 8 = 20$
- Denominator: $\sqrt{10 \cdot 40} = \sqrt{400} = 20$
- $r = 20 / 20 = 1.0$ → **perfectly correlated** → drop $X_2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Feature Engineering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset CSV / DB] --> B[Exploratory Data Analysis EDA]
    B --> C{Detect Missing Values?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Imputation Mean Median Mode KNN]
    C -- No --> E[Detect Outliers IQR Z-score]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Outliers Present?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Robust Scaler or Winsorization]
    F -- No --> H[Feature Creation Domain Math Temporal]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Categorical Variables Detected]
    I --> J{Low Cardinality k less 15?}
    J -- Yes --> K[One Hot Encoding]
    J -- No --> L[Target Encoding or Frequency Encoding]
    K --> M[Numerical Scaling Min Max or Z score]
    L --> M
    H --> M
    M --> N{Skewed Distribution?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Log or Box Cox Transform]
    N -- No --> P[Feature Selection Filter Wrapper Embedded]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final Feature Matrix X]
    Q --> R[Train ML Model Logistic Regression Random Forest]
    R --> S[Evaluate Accuracy F1 AUC]
    S --> T{Performance Satisfactory?}
    T -- No --> B
    T -- Yes --> U[Deploy Analytical Model]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Selecting Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Transformation Technique] --> B{Data Type}
    B -- Numerical Continuous --> C{Distribution Shape}
    B -- Categorical --> D{Cardinality}
    B -- Date Time --> E[Temporal Feature Extraction]
    C -- Normal --> F{Model Family}
    C -- Skewed Right --> G[Log or Box Cox]
    C -- Skewed Left --> H[Square or Cube Transform]
    C -- Heavy Tails --> I[Robust Scaler]
    F -- Distance Based --> J[Standardization Z score]
    F -- Tree Based --> K[No Scaling Required]
    F -- Neural Network --> L[Min Max 0 1]
    D -- Low --> M[One Hot Encoding]
    D -- High --> N[Target or Frequency Encoding]
    E --> O[Day Month Year Hour Is Weekend]
    G --> P[Apply Transform]
    H --> P
    I --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Feature Engineering Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Raw Dataframe]
        I2[Schema Metadata]
        I3[Domain Rules KB]
    end

    subgraph FE_Engine[Feature Engineering Engine]
        F1[Cleaner Module]
        F2[Creator Module]
        F3[Transformer Module]
        F4[Selector Module]
        F5[Validator Module]
    end

    subgraph Output
        O1[Transformed Feature Matrix X]
        O2[Pipeline Pickle File]
        O3[Feature Importance Report]
    end

    subgraph Consumer
        C1[ML Trainer]
        C2[Inference API]
        C3[Dashboard]
    end

    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F1
    I3 --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F5
    F5 --> O1
    F5 --> O2
    F5 --> O3
    O1 --> C1
    O2 --> C2
    O3 --> C3
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define feature engineering. List any four techniques used for feature transformation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Feature Engineering** is the process of creating new features and transforming existing features from raw data using domain knowledge to improve the performance of machine learning models.

**Four feature transformation techniques:**
1. **Min-Max Normalization** — scales features to $[0, 1]$.
2. **Z-Score Standardization** — centers to mean $0$, scales to std $1$.
3. **Log Transform** — reduces right-skewness.
4. **One-Hot Encoding** — converts categorical variables into binary numeric columns.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for techniques)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Min-Max Scaling and Z-Score Standardization. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Min-Max Scaling | Z-Score Standardization |
|--------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Formula | $\dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output Range | $[0, 1]$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | **High** | Moderate |
| Preferred For | Neural Networks, Image pixels | SVM, Logistic Regression, PCA |
| Assumes Gaussian | No | Implicitly yes |

**When Preferred:**
- Use **Min-Max** when bounded input is required (e.g., activation functions in neural networks).
- Use **Z-Score** when data is normally distributed or for distance/gradient-based algorithms.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Min-Max Normalization** technique with its mathematical formulation. Given the dataset $X = \{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$, apply Min-Max normalization and show the transformed values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **feature creation techniques** in detail with at least three real-world examples from healthcare, finance, and e-commerce domains. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (a):

**Step 1 — State the Formula.**

$$X' = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Min and Max from the Given Dataset.**

Given $X = \{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$, we have:
- $X_{\min} = 2$
- $X_{\max} = 14$
- Range = $X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 12$

**Step 3 — Apply the Transformation.**

$$X_1' = \dfrac{2 - 2}{12} = 0.0000$$
$$X_2' = \dfrac{5 - 2}{12} = 0.2500$$
$$X_3' = \dfrac{8 - 2}{12} = 0.5000$$
$$X_4' = \dfrac{11 - 2}{12} = 0.7500$$
$$X_5' = \dfrac{14 - 2}{12} = 1.0000$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**
All transformed values lie in $[0, 1]$, confirming correct application. The relative ordering is preserved.

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (b):

**Feature Creation** is the process of generating new informative features from raw data using domain knowledge, mathematical operations, or temporal decomposition.

**Example 1 — Healthcare:**
- Raw features: `weight` (kg), `height` (m)
- Created feature: $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{\text{weight}}{\text{height}^2}$
- **Why useful:** BMI is a clinically validated indicator of obesity and cardiovascular risk, far more predictive than weight or height alone.

**Example 2 — Finance / Banking:**
- Raw features: `annual_income`, `existing_debt`, `credit_history_years`
- Created feature: $\text{DTI Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{existing_debt}}{\text{annual\_income}}$
- **Why useful:** Debt-to-Income ratio is the most critical predictor of loan default risk per Basel III banking norms.

**Example 3 — E-Commerce / Retail:**
- Raw features: `last_purchase_date`, `total_orders`, `total_spent`
- Created feature: **RFM Score** combining Recency (days since last purchase), Frequency (total orders), Monetary (total spent).
- **Why useful:** RFM segmentation enables targeted marketing and customer churn prediction.

**Additional techniques:** Polynomial features ($X^2, X^3$), interaction terms ($X_1 \times X_2$), and date decomposition (day-of-week, month, is_holiday).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Z-Score Standardization** formula and apply it to the dataset $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$. Verify that the transformed data has mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Filter, Wrapper, and Embedded** methods of feature selection with one example technique for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (a):

**Step 1 — State the Goal.**
Standardize $X$ such that the transformed variable $Z$ has $\mu_Z = 0$ and $\sigma_Z = 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply Centering.**
Subtract the mean:

$$X_c = X - \mu$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mean and Standard Deviation of the Given Dataset.**
For $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$:

$$\mu = \dfrac{10 + 20 + 30 + 40 + 50}{5} = \dfrac{150}{5} = 30$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \mu)^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400 + 100 + 0 + 100 + 400}{4}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{4}} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.811$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Z-Score Formula.**

$$Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

| $X_i$ | $X_i - 30$ | $Z_i = (X_i - 30)/15.811$ |
|-------|------------|---------------------------|
| 10 | $-20$ | $-1.265$ |
| 20 | $-10$ | $-0.632$ |
| 30 | $0$ | $0.000$ |
| 40 | $+10$ | $+0.632$ |
| 50 | $+20$ | $+1.265$ |

**Step 5 — Verify Mean of $Z$.**

$$\mu_Z = \dfrac{-1.265 - 0.632 + 0 + 0.632 + 1.265}{5} = \dfrac{0}{5} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Verify Standard Deviation of $Z$.**

$$\sigma_Z = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.600 + 0.400 + 0 + 0.400 + 1.600}{4}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.000}{4}} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

The transformation is verified.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (b):

| Aspect | Filter Methods | Wrapper Methods | Embedded Methods |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Statistical test on features independent of model | Use a model to score feature subsets | Feature selection built into model training |
| **Computational Cost** | **Low** | **High** (retrain per subset) | Moderate |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low | High | Low–Moderate |
| **Example** | Pearson Correlation, Chi-Square, ANOVA | Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), Forward/Backward Selection | Lasso (L1 Regularization), Tree-based Feature Importance |
| **Formula Example** | Drop if $\vert r_{ij} \vert \geq 0.85$ | $\min_{S \subseteq F} \text{Loss}(S)$ via search | $\min \sum L + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ |

**Detailed Notes:**

- **Filter (e.g., Pearson Correlation):** Fast, scalable, ignores feature interactions. Best for **preliminary pruning**.
- **Wrapper (e.g., RFE):** Trains the model repeatedly, eliminating weakest feature each iteration. Captures interactions but is **computationally expensive**.
- **Embedded (e.g., Lasso):** Performs selection *during* training via L1 penalty. Provides sparse, interpretable models.

**Conclusion:** In practice, **embedded methods** (Lasso, tree importance) offer the best trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark Deduction Areas in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Formula Recall Errors (–2 marks):** Students often write $X' = \dfrac{X}{\sigma}$ instead of $\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$, forgetting the centering term. **Always show both steps.**
> 2. **Confusing Min-Max with Standardization (–1 mark):** Clearly mention the **output range** $[0,1]$ vs $(-\infty, \infty)$.
> 3. **Skipping Numerical Verification (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award marks for the **"verified"** step. Always re-compute $\mu$ and $\sigma$ after transformation.
> 4. **Listing but not Explaining (–1 to –2 marks):** For 7-mark sub-parts, listing techniques without explanation yields only 3–4 marks. Always include **why** and **when**.
> 5. **Not Presenting Domain Examples (–2 marks):** For "feature creation" questions, citing **only mathematical examples** is incomplete. Include at least **one domain-driven** example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feature Engineering = Feature Creation + Feature Transformation + Feature Selection**.
- **Min-Max Scaling** maps data to $[0, 1]$; **Z-Score Standardization** centers at $0$ with unit variance.
- **Tree-based models** (Random Forest, XGBoost) do **not** require scaling; **distance-based models** (KNN, SVM, K-Means) **do**.
- **Log Transform** is the first remedy for right-skewed positive data; **Box-Cox** auto-tunes $\lambda$.
- **One-Hot Encoding** is preferred for nominal categories; **Label Encoding** for ordinal categories; **Target Encoding** for high-cardinality.
- **Pearson correlation threshold $\geq 0.85$** is the standard KTU rule for dropping redundant features.
- **L1 (Lasso)** regularization performs automatic feature selection by shrinking coefficients to **0**.
- **Robust Scaler** uses median and IQR — preferred when **outliers** are present.
- **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** features are the gold standard for customer analytics.
- **Time-based features** (day-of-week, month, is_weekend) often capture seasonality missed by raw date columns.
- **Final verification step** (re-computing mean and standard deviation after standardization) is mandatory for full marks in KTU exams.
- Always choose transformation based on the **algorithm family** — this is the most-tested KTU concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Data Visualization with Matplotlib and Seaborn
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET865 - DATA ANALYTICS AND MACHINE LEARNING WITH PYTHON > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Data Visualization with Matplotlib and Seaborn ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Visualization** is the graphical representation of information and data. By using visual elements like charts, graphs, and maps, data visualization tools provide an accessible way to see and understand trends, outliers, and patterns in data. In the context of Python data analytics, two libraries dominate the ecosystem: - **Matplotlib** is the foundational, low-level plotting library in Python. It was created by **John D. Hunter (2003)** and provides a MATLAB-like interface through `pyplot`. It offers full control over every element of a figure (axes, ticks, labels, annotations) and is the base upon which most higher-level libraries (including Seaborn) are built. - **Seaborn** is a high-level statistical data visualization library built on top of Matplotlib. It was created by **Michael Waskom** and provides a more concise, declarative API for producing attractive and statistically informative plots, especially when working with **pandas DataFrames**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 emphasizes the **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** phase, where visualization acts as the bridge between raw tabular data and actionable insights. Matplotlib and Seaborn are the two prescribed tools. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intuition Layer" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Doctor and the X-Ray:** > Imagine a doctor receiving a patient's report. One report is a wall of numbers (hemoglobin = 13.2, WBC = 7400, platelets = 250000...). The other is an **X-ray image**. The X-ray lets the doctor *see* the broken rib in a single glance. **Matplotlib is like the X-ray machine** — it gives you the raw imaging power. **Seaborn is like the radiology specialist** — it knows which views to take, how to highlight fractures, and how to label anatomy so a general physician can read it instantly. In engineering terms: - **Matplotlib** = the **assembly language** of plotting. Maximum control, minimum abstraction. - **Seaborn** = a **domain-specific language (DSL)** for statistical graphics. Less code, more semantic meaning. ### 1.3 Why Data Visualization Matters in Machine Learning Workflows | Stage of ML Pipeline | Role of Visualization | |---|---| | **Data Understanding (EDA)** | Histograms, boxplots, pairplots reveal distributions and outliers | | **Feature Engineering** | Correlation heatmaps guide feature selection | | **Model Diagnosis** | Residual plots, ROC curves, confusion matrices | | **Result Communication** | Stakeholder-friendly plots for reports and dashboards | | **Anomaly Detection** | Scatter plots expose clusters and noise visually | ### 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU Board Definitions) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Figure**: The top-level container that holds everything you draw. Think of it as the **canvas**. > - **Axes**: The actual plotting area where data is rendered. A Figure can contain multiple Axes. (Not to be confused with the word "axis" — a single x-axis or y-axis.) > - **Artist**: Everything you can see on a Figure is an Artist — Text, Line2D, Patch, etc. > - **Backend**: The rendering engine (e.g., `Agg`, `TkAgg`, `MacOSX`) that converts your code into pixels on screen or file. > - **Pyplot interface**: A **state-based** (MATLAB-style) interface where a single implicit "current figure" is tracked. > - **Object-Oriented (OO) interface**: An **explicit** interface where you instantiate Figure and Axes objects and call methods on them. > - **Figure-level function** (Seaborn): Returns a `FacetGrid` or `JointGrid` — manages its own Figure. > - **Axes-level function** (Seaborn): Plots onto a *given* Axes, giving you full control over layout. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 The Architecture of Matplotlib — A Three-Layer Stack Matplotlib is engineered in **three distinct conceptual layers**: 1. **Backend Layer** (`matplotlib.backend_bases`) - Handles the low-level rendering (drawing on screen, writing to PNG/PDF/SVG files). - Examples: `Agg` (non-interactive, file output), `TkAgg` (interactive window), `nbAgg` (Jupyter Notebook). - You typically do not interact with this layer directly. 2. **Artist Layer** (`matplotlib.artist.Artist`) - Everything visible on the chart is an `Artist` object. - Two main types: **Primitive Artists** (`Line2D`, `Rectangle`, `Text`) and **Composite Artists** (`Axes`, `Axis`, `Figure`). 3. **Scripting Layer** (`matplotlib.pyplot`) - The thin, user-facing wrapper that most beginners use. - It maintains an internal state machine so that `plt.plot()` automatically draws on the "current" figure. ### 2.2 Why Two Interfaces Exist in Matplotlib > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between `plt.plot()` and `ax.plot()`. Understand this deeply. | Feature | Pyplot Interface (`plt.*`) | Object-Oriented Interface (`fig, ax = plt.subplots()`) | |---|---|---| | **Style** | Stateful, implicit | Explicit, object-based | | **Best For** | Quick exploratory plots | Complex multi-panel figures | | **Control Level** | Low-to-Medium | High | | **Reproducibility** | Lower (depends on hidden state) | Higher | | **KTU Recommendation** | Use for one-liners | **Preferred** for any figure with > 1 subplot | ### 2.3 Seaborn's Design Philosophy Seaborn was designed with three principles: - **Integrated with pandas**: Accepts DataFrames and column names directly. - **Statistical awareness**: Automatically computes and displays confidence intervals, KDEs, and regression lines. - **Aesthetic defaults**: Beautiful color palettes and themes out-of-the-box. Seaborn internally calls Matplotlib's API to do the actual drawing. Therefore **every Seaborn plot is, ultimately, a Matplotlib plot** — meaning all Matplotlib customization techniques still apply. ### 2.4 KTU Reference Sheet — Core Plot Types and Their Use Cases | Plot Type | Matplotlib Function | Seaborn Function | Primary Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Line Plot** | `plt.plot()` | `sns.lineplot()` | Trends over time / continuous x | | **Scatter Plot** | `plt.scatter()` | `sns.scatterplot()` | Relationship between two variables | | **Bar Plot** | `plt.bar()` | `sns.barplot()` | Compare categorical means | | **Histogram** | `plt.hist()` | `sns.histplot()` | Distribution of a single variable | | **Box Plot** | `plt.boxplot()` | `sns.boxplot()` | Distribution + outliers by category | | **Violin Plot** | — | `sns.violinplot()` | Distribution shape + density | | **Heatmap** | `plt.imshow()` | `sns.heatmap()` | Correlation / matrix data | | **Pair Plot** | — | `sns.pairplot()` | All pairwise relationships in one grid | | **Count Plot** | — | `sns.countplot()` | Frequency of categorical values | | **KDE Plot** | — | `sns.kdeplot()` | Smoothed density estimate | ### 2.5 KTU Reference Sheet — Essential Customization Parameters | Parameter | Applies To | Effect | Example Value | |---|---|---|---| | `figsize` | `plt.subplots(figsize=(w, h))` | Width and height in inches | `(10, 6)` | | `color` | Most plots | Stroke/fill color | `'steelblue'` or `'#1f77b4'` | | `linestyle` | Line plots | Solid, dashed, dotted | `'-'`, `'--'`, `':'` | | `linewidth` | Line plots | Thickness in points | `2.0` | | `marker` | Line/scatter | Point shape | `'o'`, `'s'`, `'^'`, `'D'` | | `alpha` | Most plots | Transparency ($0$ to $1$) | `0.7` | | `cmap` | Scatter/imshow | Colormap name | `'viridis'`, `'plasma'` | | `bins` | Histogram | Number of bins | `30` | | `hue` | Seaborn | Color by category | `column_name` | | `style` | Seaborn | Marker by category | `column_name` | | `size` | Seaborn | Point size by value | `column_name` | | `palette` | Seaborn | Color palette | `'Set2'`, `'coolwarm'` | | `gridspec_kw` | `plt.subplots()` | Subplot layout | `{'width_ratios': [2, 1]}` | | `tight_layout()` | Figure | Auto-adjust spacing | `plt.tight_layout()` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting `plt.tight_layout()` is the **#1 reason** students lose marks on visualization questions. Overlapping axis labels and titles are an immediate deduction in board exams. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Civil Engineering**: Visualizing stress-strain curves from material testing. - **Electrical Engineering**: Plotting signal waveforms and FFT spectra. - **Biomedical**: ECG/EKG signal visualization. - **IoT Systems**: Real-time temperature/humidity dashboards. - **Finance**: Stock candlestick charts (using `mplfinance`). ## 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Section 3 — Hands-On Code Lab.** Since PEIET865 is a CS/AI course (Data Analytics and Machine Learning with Python), Python code is the primary analytical artifact. Each code block is fully executable and annotated line-by-line. ### 3.1 Lab 1 — Your First Matplotlib Figure (Pyplot Style) ```python import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np # Step 1: Generate sample data — 50 evenly spaced points between 0 and 2*pi x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 50) y = np.sin(x) # Step 2: Call the pyplot plotting function (state-based) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4)) # Create an 8x4 inch figure canvas plt.plot(x, y, color='steelblue', linestyle='-', linewidth=2, marker='o', label='sin(x)') # Step 3: Add labels, title, and legend plt.title("Sine Wave — First Plot", fontsize=14) plt.xlabel("Angle (radians)") plt.ylabel("Amplitude") plt.legend(loc='upper right') plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5) # Step 4: Render plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` **Logic Walkthrough:** | Line | What It Does | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | `np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 50)` | Creates 50 x-values | Smooth curve needs dense sampling | | `plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))` | Allocates canvas in inches | Controls aspect ratio | | `plt.plot(...)` | Draws the Line2D artist onto current Axes | Core drawing primitive | | `plt.legend()` | Reads the `label=` kwarg from `plt.plot()` | Connects data to identity | | `plt.tight_layout()` | Auto-pads subplot borders | Prevents label cutoff | ### 3.2 Lab 2 — The Object-Oriented (OO) Interface ```python # Step 1: Create Figure and Axes explicitly in one call fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4)) # Step 2: Plot by calling .plot() on the Axes object ax.plot(x, np.sin(x), label='sin(x)', color='crimson') ax.plot(x, np.cos(x), label='cos(x)', color='teal', linestyle='--') # Step 3: Set methods ON the axes object (not plt) ax.set_title("Trigonometric Functions") ax.set_xlabel("Angle (radians)") ax.set_ylabel("Value") ax.legend() ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question says "using the object-oriented interface," you **must** use `fig, ax = plt.subplots()` and then call `ax.set_xlabel()` (not `plt.xlabel()`). Mixing both styles in the same answer is a common deduction. ### 3.3 Lab 3 — Seaborn's Statistical Edge ```python import seaborn as sns import pandas as pd # Step 1: Load the built-in 'tips' dataset tips = sns.load_dataset('tips') # Step 2: One-line scatter plot with hue, size, and style encoding sns.scatterplot( data=tips, x='total_bill', y='tip', hue='time', # Color by Lunch/Dinner style='smoker', # Marker shape by smoker status size='party_size', # Point size by group size palette='Set2' ) plt.title("Tips vs Total Bill — Seaborn Statistical Scatter") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` **Why Seaborn beats Matplotlib here:** - It accepts a **DataFrame and column names** directly — no manual column extraction. - The `hue`, `style`, `size` semantics encode a **four-dimensional** story in one plot. ### 3.4 Lab 4 — The Heatmap (Correlation Matrix) ```python # Step 1: Compute Pearson correlation matrix for numeric columns corr = tips[['total_bill', 'tip', 'size']].corr() # Step 2: Render the heatmap sns.heatmap( corr, annot=True, # Print numeric values inside cells fmt='.2f', # Two decimal places cmap='coolwarm', # Diverging colormap (red = negative, blue = positive) vmin=-1, vmax=1 # Lock the color scale to correlation range ) plt.title("Correlation Heatmap") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ### 3.5 Lab 5 — Subplots (Multi-Panel Figures) ```python fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4)) # Left panel — histogram of total bill axes[0].hist(tips['total_bill'], bins=20, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black') axes[0].set_title("Distribution of Total Bill") axes[0].set_xlabel("Total Bill ($)") # Right panel — boxplot of tip by day sns.boxplot(data=tips, x='day', y='tip', ax=axes[1]) axes[1].set_title("Tip Amount by Day") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice:** `sns.boxplot(..., ax=axes[1])` — passing the `ax` argument forces Seaborn to draw on a **specific Axes**. This is the canonical pattern for embedding Seaborn inside a Matplotlib subplot grid. ### 3.6 Lab 6 — Saving Figures for Reports ```python fig.savefig('my_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') # dpi=300 ensures print-quality resolution # bbox_inches='tight' crops excess whitespace ``` Supported formats: `.png`, `.jpg`, `.pdf`, `.svg`, `.eps`. ### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Library Should I Use? | Scenario | Recommended Library | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Quick exploratory one-liner | Matplotlib (`plt`) | Less boilerplate | | Publication-quality multi-panel figure | Matplotlib (OO) | Maximum layout control | | Statistical visualization with hue/style | **Seaborn** | Concise semantic API | | Heatmap / correlation matrix | **Seaborn** | Purpose-built | | Pairwise exploration of many variables | **Seaborn (`pairplot`)** | One-liner solution | | Custom 3D or animated plots | Matplotlib | Seaborn has no 3D support | | Integration with pandas DataFrames | **Seaborn** | Native DataFrame support | | Tight integration with NumPy arrays | Matplotlib | Native array support | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Matplotlib's Three-Layer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB user["User Code: plt.plot or ax.plot"] scripting["Scripting Layer: matplotlib.pyplot"] artist["Artist Layer: Figure, Axes, Line2D, Text"] backend["Backend Layer: Agg, TkAgg, nbAgg"] output["Output: Screen Window PNG PDF SVG"] user --> scripting scripting --> artist artist --> backend backend --> output ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Figure and Axes Containment Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB fig["Figure - Top-level canvas"] ax1["Axes - Plotting area 1"] ax2["Axes - Plotting area 2"] title["Title - Text Artist"] xlabel["X-axis Label"] ylabel["Y-axis Label"] line1["Line2D - The data line"] legend["Legend - Patch Artists"] fig --> ax1 fig --> ax2 ax1 --> title ax1 --> xlabel ax1 --> ylabel ax1 --> line1 ax1 --> legend ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Seaborn's Position in the Python Visualization Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB pandas["Pandas DataFrame"] seaborn["Seaborn - High-level statistical API"] matplotlib["Matplotlib - Mid-level drawing engine"] backend["Rendering Backend - Agg / TkAgg"] screen["Pixels on Screen or File on Disk"] pandas --> seaborn seaborn --> matplotlib matplotlib --> backend backend --> screen ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Standard EDA Visualization Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR load["1 Load Data
pd.read_csv"] inspect["2 Inspect
df.head df.info"] univariate["3 Univariate
Histograms Boxplots"] bivariate["4 Bivariate
Scatter Heatmap"] multivariate["5 Multivariate
Pairplot sns.pairplot"] insight["6 Insight
Document findings"] load --> inspect --> univariate --> bivariate --> multivariate --> insight ``` ### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Interface Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Need to plot data"] q1{"Multiple subplots
or fine control?"} q2{"Statistical encoding
hue style size?"} q3{"Quick one-liner
exploration?"} pyplot["Use pyplot style: plt.plot"] oo["Use Object-Oriented: fig ax = plt.subplots"] sns["Use Seaborn: sns.scatterplot etc"] start --> q1 q1 -- No --> q3 q1 -- Yes --> oo q3 -- Yes --> pyplot q3 -- No --> q2 q2 -- Yes --> sns q2 -- No --> oo ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between the pyplot interface and the Object-Oriented interface of Matplotlib. When would you prefer the OO interface?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Pyplot Interface | Object-Oriented Interface | |---|---|---| | Syntax style | Stateful, implicit current figure | Explicit Figure and Axes objects | | Example | `plt.plot(x, y)` | `fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot(x, y)` | | Control | Limited | Full control over each Axes | | Best use | Quick exploratory plots | Multi-panel or publication figures | **(1 mark)** for stating the syntax difference, **(1 mark)** for control difference, **(1 mark)** for the use-case preference (multi-subplot / publication). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **List any three Seaborn plot functions and state one use case for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **`sns.heatmap()`** — Used to visualize correlation matrices and missing-data patterns. (1 mark) 2. **`sns.pairplot()`** — Plots all pairwise scatter plots and histograms for a DataFrame in one call. (1 mark) 3. **`sns.boxplot()`** — Displays the five-number summary and outliers grouped by a categorical variable. (1 mark) *(Acceptable alternates: `sns.violinplot`, `sns.countplot`, `sns.kdeplot`, `sns.barplot`, `sns.histplot` — any three with valid use cases earn full marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Matplotlib-Focused** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]** With the help of a suitable Python program, explain the steps to create a line plot using Matplotlib's Object-Oriented interface.
